WASHINGTON — The federal government will hold the first lease sale for commercial offshore wind energy projects at the end of July, the Interior Departmentannouncedon Tuesday.
Secretary of the Interior Sally Jewell wants to “strengthen our nation’s competitiveness.”
The sale will offer164,750 acresof federal waters off the coasts of Rhode Island and Massachusetts. If that is fully developed, officials said, it could produce as much as 3,400 megawatts of electricity, enough to power more than one million homes.
The lease sale shows the Obama administration’s determination to pursue a wide range of domestic energy production, from fossil fuels and renewable sources. Sally Jewell, thenew secretary of the interior, said the department would accelerate offshore wind leasing if the July 31 lease sale was successful.
“Today we are moving closer to tapping into the enormous potential offered by offshore wind to create jobs, increase our sustainability and strengthen our nation’s competitiveness in this new energy frontier,” Ms. Jewell said in a statement. “As we experience record domestic oil and gas development, we are also working to ensure that America leads the world in developing the energy of the future.”
Ms. Jewell’s predecessor, Ken Salazar, started the push toward Atlantic Coast wind energy in 2010, identifying a number of sites with significant potential. Federal, state and private officials have also begun planning a major undersea transmission line known as theAtlantic Wind Connectionto carry power from offshore turbines to onshore distribution centers.
The first federal lease sale will be limited to nine bidders who have met the technical, legal and financial qualifications to build offshore turbine farms. The Interior Department will also consider whether the wind developers have negotiated agreements with utilities to buy wind-generated power.
The department conducted anenvironmental assessmentof the impacts of major offshore wind installations and said it found no significant impact. But some opponents have claimed that the turbines, which can reach as high as 440 feet above the ocean surface, are unsightly, dangerous to birds and a hazard to navigation.
Representative Edward J. Markey, Democrat of Massachusetts, said offshore wind projects would benefit the economy while producing low-carbon energy.
“Offshore wind is a win for American jobs, for American energy security and for our environment,” Mr. Markey said, “and it will start off the coast of New England.”
In 2010, federal officials approved the fiercely contested offshore wind farm known asCape Windafter nine years of battles with Massachusetts residents, including members of the Kennedy family, who objected to the giant turbines off the coast of Nantucket.
The Cape Wind project, on which construction is scheduled to begin this year, will cover 24 square miles and lie about 5.2 miles from the nearest shore, on the mainland, and about 13.8 miles from Nantucket Island.
About a dozen other privately financedoffshore wind projectsare in various stages of development.
Affirmative action hangs on, but the court has set a very high bar.
Most of us need to be more active, but, Room for Debate asks, how do you know when that healthy habit has crossed the line?

Greg Anderson
Nic Kerdiles made his college debut in late November, delayed nearly two months because of an N.C.A.A. suspension, and the Badgers have not lost since.
Unlike many North American sports, hockey players can choose to develop outside of an educational setting, in the Canadian Hockey League, though some contend that the on- and off-ice value of a college experience makes it the clear choice for most players.
It’s an argument supported by the sharp rise in the number of former college players in the N.H.L. (more than 300 in 2011-12), and the percentage of players who earn their college degrees (85 percent in the latest N.C.A.A. study).
Depending on the university, a hockey scholarship can be worth $200,000 or more in real tuition savings over a four-year period. But the real value is perhaps best captured by the actions of the
Wisconsin freshman forward Nic Kerdiles
.
Kerdiles made his college debut in late November, delayed nearly two months because of an N.C.A.A. suspension for a minor infraction involving a family adviser that temporarily affected his amateur status.
Kerdiles, a native of Irvine, Calif., had other options, but he never wavered in his commitment to college hockey.
“I’ll be honest with you, no, there was never a doubt,” Kerdiles said when asked if the N.C.A.A. suspension made him consider leaving Wisconsin. “This campus is amazing. The facilities are unbelievable. The coaching staff is excellent and my teammates are great. It was really an easy decision for my family and me.”
The Badgers have not lost since Kerdiles first suited up (7-0-3 after last weekend’s sweep at Minnesota State). He has two goals and five assists.
In the long run, however, Kerdiles figures to be the real winner, and his actions are far more powerful than any bullet points, percentages, or dollar values can be in placing a value on the scholarship a hockey player receives.
“It is my turn to be a role model for young hockey players in California and the West Coast,” he said. “I take that responsibility very seriously and would be devastated to see any of them change their path from college route just because of the adversity I faced as a student-athlete.”
Mike Snee is the executive director of College Hockey, Inc., a nonprofit organization that promotes the value of the college hockey path for talented young players and recently celebrated its third anniversary.

New York voters on Tuesday approved a constitutional amendment to expand casino gambling, authorizing as many as seven full-scale casinos as part of a plan meant to bring jobs to economically distressed upstate regions.
Follow us onTwitterand like us onFacebookfor news and conversation.
The proposal, which was championed by Gov.Andrew M. Cuomo, was supported by 57 percent of voters, with most precincts reporting.
The approval is a milestone in the gambling industry’slong, expensive pushto tap into the New York market, an effort that has spanned decades, cost tens of millions of dollars and is certain to continue as gambling companies vie for the right to develop the new casinos.
New York State already has five Indian-run casinos, all of them upstate, and nine slot machine parlors at racetracks. And the State Legislature, at the urging of Mr. Cuomo, has required that at first, only four new casinos will be permitted, and only upstate: in the Albany area, the Catskills-Hudson Valley region and part of the Southern Tier, a region along the northern border of Pennsylvania.
Mr. Cuomo, a Democrat, has said that casino development would create jobs, lure tourists and allow the state to recapture some of the gambling revenue now flowing to nearby states with casinos.
“We literally hemorrhage people from the borders who go to casinos,” Mr. Cuomo told reporters after voting in Westchester County on Tuesday. “I think it will keep the money in this state, and I think it’s a major economic development vehicle for the Hudson Valley especially and for upstate New York.”
Voters who marked their ballots in support of the proposed amendment said the potential financial gain was too significant for the state to pass up, especially given the slumbering economy in parts of upstate.
“We really need something to come in and revitalize this area,” Jackie Burris, a retired special education teacher who has a second home in the Catskills hamlet of Rock Hill, said after voting in Great Neck. “We need it desperately.”
New York’s debate over casinos was far more muted than in other places where casinos have been proposed, in part because Mr. Cuomo drafted the casino plan in a way that neutralized many likely opponents, like the Indian tribes that run the upstate casinos.
The casino amendment was promoted by a coalition called New York Jobs Now, which was primarily financed by gambling interests, including operators of the racetrack slot machine parlors, as well as labor unions that stood to benefit from the development of casinos. The coalition raised more than $4 million for the effort, which included a series of television ads promoting the economic benefits of casino development and citing endorsements from newspaper editorial boards.
The supporters of the measure benefited from the wording on the ballot, which portrayed the proposalin a positive light, suggesting that it would lead to new jobs, increased school financing and property tax relief, and omitting any negative arguments. The wording, adopted by the State Board of Elections after consultation with Mr. Cuomo, drew criticism from government watchdog groups, but a lawsuit challenging itwas unsuccessful.
A disparate collection of opponents tried to persuade voters to oppose the casino expansion, but their efforts were largely homespun. One group of opponents paid for a last-minute, less-than-polishedtelevision commercialquoting former Gov. Mario M. Cuomo denouncing casino gambling two decades ago, but opponents generally had little money to back up their efforts. (In response to the ad, Mario Cuomo endorsed the referendum, saying, “A great deal has changed in 20 years.”)
The opponents argued that Andrew Cuomo and other supporters of the proposal were being overly optimistic about the economic benefits, citing the struggles of destinations like Atlantic City as casinos have proliferated on the East Coast.
The state’s Catholic bishopsurged votersto “consider the potential for negative consequences” and the Episcopal bishop of New Yorkwarned against“false hopes,” saying, “In places where casino gambling has been introduced, almost all gains have come at the high social cost of addiction and family disintegration, and deepening poverty.”
The push to expand gambling was a textbook case of how well-financed interests can push Albany to embrace an industry, hiring an army of lobbyists and wooing Mr. Cuomo and lawmakers with millions of dollars in campaign contributions.
The new casino licenses will be awarded by a panel to be appointed by theState Gaming Commission. Developers have expressed the most interestin the Catskills, where large resorts once prospered.
Angela Macropoulos and Julie Turkewitz contributed reporting.

The author boards an XOJet craft at Teterboro Airport, in New Jersey.
In less time than it takes to undergo a body scan, I breezed through the terminal and onto the tarmac.
From apps to cruises, packing to going solo, the Getaway columnist covers a diverse set of travel topics.Follow @stephronyt on Twitter
Follow@nytimestravelfor tips, features and photography from all over the globe.
No one at Teterboro Airport, in low-slung industrial New Jersey, asked for my driverâs license. No one rifled through my bag. There were no screaming children or grown-ups in pajama bottoms wheeling luggage the size of fat steamer trunks. It was strangely serene: only the sound of the wind and the tap of my heels on the runway as I walked toward two pilots at the foot of a Challenger 300, a gleaming private jet with seating for nine. I stepped onto a swatch of blue carpet beneath the air stair and, steadied by a pilotâs hand, at long last boarded a plane like a human being, not a pack mule.
Inside, the pilot in command, Rob Martin ofXOJet, a private jet company based in San Francisco, went over the essentials: the iPod dock; the touch screen to control the lights and movies; the leather swivel seats that I was told (while treating mine like a Tilt-a-Whirl) cost $30,000 to replace; the satellite phone; the Nespresso machine; the cabinet with the Oreo cookies and Kistler chardonnay.
âOne thing I forgot to mention,â Mr. Martin said before we took off, âthe couch will fold out into a bed.â
At a time when industry surveys show that travelers are fed up with epic lines at commercial airports, when lounges are overflowing with airline-branded credit card holders, and first class is but a shadow of what it was in the golden age of air travel, companies are making private jets easier to come by. What had been an industry that relied on full or partial ownership of planes is opening up, with jet operators and owners like XOJet offering more flexible programs, and brokers who donât own planes working in tandem with them to offer seats â in some cases through apps â within striking distance of the price of a first class ticket.
âThe industry has literally changed 180 degrees in the last five years,â said Bill Papariella, an aviation executive who worked at NetJets, Marquis Jet, and Sentient before becoming a founder and president of the operatorJet Edge International. Companies that survived the recession have made pricing simpler and now offer more membership options, based on where, when and how often you fly. And a handful of new industry players are making booking a private jet as easy as ordering up a private car on Uber.
âItâs much easier and much cheaper than itâs ever been before,â said Bradley Stewart, chief executive of XOJet.
Even so, can you afford to travel like James Bond? The answer depends on what type of flier you are. (How often do you fly? Where do you fly? How rigid is your schedule?) Different companies have different pricing structures, but one of the most common models is a yearly or monthly membership fee plus the cost of your flights. That can run you anywhere from several thousand dollars a year to several hundred thousand dollars a year.
At one end of the spectrum are operators like Jet Edge International, who say their private jets are the purview of those with net worth in excess of $50 million. âWe specialize in the 1 percent of the 1 percent,â Mr. Papariella said.
At the other end of the spectrum are a handful of start-ups that want to change that, likeBlackJet, which is enabling first-class fliers to graduate to private travel by selling individual seats to its members, who currently pay a $2,500 annual fee. The company, which began putting clients on flights late last year, finds jet owners or operators that will transport 6 to 14 travelers at a time in markets like New York, South Florida, Los Angeles, San Francisco, Las Vegas (and next up, Chicago and Washington, D.C.).
âOur client is the mass affluent as opposed to the 1 percent of the population,â said Dean Rotchin, founder and chief executive of BlackJet. âItâs bringing it from the rock star level down to a practical tool for the mass affluent.â
For instance, a recent search for a last-minute, one-way first-class ticket on a commercial airline from New York to Los Angeles was about $1,400 to $2,000. On BlackJet, Mr. Rotchin said that trip would be about $3,600, in addition to the membership fee. (There are no TSA lines but passengersâ names are checked against no-fly lists.)
You must be flexible, though. When you book a flight on BlackJet (which can sell individual seats because as a broker it does not need an FAA operating license), you choose either a departure in the a.m. (between 7 and 10) or p.m. (between 4 and 7). Also, the amenities on the jets vary.

Getting to the bottom of HealthCare.gov’s flop.

TheFood and Drug Administrationismovingto ban virtually all artificial trans fats from the American food supply. This long-awaited move should save thousands of lives and potentially billions of dollars in medical and economic costs a year.
For Op-Ed, follow@nytopinionand to hear from the editorial page
editor, Andrew Rosenthal, follow@andyrNYT.
Trans fats, also called partially hydrogenated vegetable oils, are made by treating vegetable oil with hydrogen gas to make it more solid. Many scientists believed, mistakenly, that trans fats were harmless or perhaps even beneficial because they substituted for saturated fats, which pose their own health risks. That turned out to be fatally wrong. Research has shown that trans fats are more dangerous than other fats.
They raise the level of “badcholesterol” in the blood and lower the level of “good cholesterol,” leading to clogged arteries, illness and death from heart disease. The Institute of Medicine concluded that there is no safe level of these fats and people should eat as little as possible. Federal health officials estimate that eliminating trans fats would prevent 20,000 heart attacks and 7,000 deaths from heart disease a year.
Most of the trans fats in the food supply have been eliminated over the past decade. Major fast-food chains dropped trans fats from their cooking oils, and food companies substituted safer fats in their products. Daily per capita consumption dropped from 4.6 grams in 2003 to one gram in 2012. But trans fats continue to be used in some processed foods, such as canned frosting, coffee creamers, and microwave popcorn, and some restaurants continue to cook with them.
The F.D.A.’s proposal would eliminate a loophole that allowed manufacturers to label their products as containing zero grams of trans fats per serving if they have less than half a gram. People who ate multiple portions of such foods could quickly take in a dangerously high level while assuming they were taking in none. The proposal is open for comment for 60 days, after which the agency will determine how fast remaining trans fats must be eliminated. Consumer groups argue persuasively that six months to a year should be enough time.

Italian police officers photographed migrants in Sicily on Tuesday.
BRUSSELS — A senior European Union official called on Tuesday for vastly expanded patrols of Mediterranean waters, as horror mounted over the deaths of hundreds of African migrants near Sicily last week.
Follow@nytimesworldfor international breaking news and headlines.
Wrecked boats on the Italian island of Lampedusa that had been used by migrants.
Immigrants were detained in a shelter in Lampedusa on Tuesday.
The bloc needs “a wide operation that will cover the whole Mediterranean from Spain to Cyprus in order to be able to prevent future catastrophes and save more lives,” Cecilia Malmstrom, the union’s commissioner for home affairs, said at a news conference after a meeting of justice and interior ministers in Luxembourg.
“I’ve asked member states to give their political support and also make available the necessary resources,” said Ms. Malmstrom, who must rely on member governments to finance and carry out search-and-rescue efforts.
The statement from Ms. Malmstrom was one of the strongest calls for a response to the disaster on Thursday, when an overstuffed and rickety trawler carrying an estimated 500 migrants fleeing war and poverty caught fire and capsized near the Italian island of Lampedusa.
Ms. Malmstrom said she would join Enrico Letta, Italy’s prime minister, and José Manuel Barroso, the president of the European Commission, on Lampedusa on Wednesday to speak with residents and victims of the shipwreck.
Divers on Tuesday retrieved another 19 bodies from the wreckage, raising the death toll to 275. The ship is submerged under 165 feet of water about a quarter-mile from Lampedusa.
Officials from the Italian Coast Guard said the death toll was expected to grow as the search continued.
Even as the European Union commissioner sought to prevent more tragedies at sea, the debate over how to treat people caught making landfall became more fraught, as the Italian authorities placed the 155 survivors of the capsizing under investigation for illegal immigration.
Under a 2009 law, illegal migrants who are not asylum seekers can face penalties for entering Italy, a situation some experts described as absurd.
“If prosecutors have the pilot and the boat, migrants are logically the victims of the smuggling,” Alberto Di Martino, a professor of criminal law and immigration at the University of Pisa, said by telephone. “They are not liable for any crime.”
On Tuesday, prosecutors in Agrigento, Sicily, said they were holding a 35-year-old Tunisian man on suspicion of being one of two men in charge of piloting the boat. Prosecutors said the man, who has not been formally charged, could face a prison sentence of more than 20 years if found guilty of multiple counts of committing manslaughter, abetting illegal immigration and causing a shipwreck.
Ms. Malmstrom said the additional patrol work in the Mediterranean should be undertaken by Frontex, the European Union border security agency, which relies on financing from the bloc’s governments to pay for surveillance equipment like planes and boats.
Ewa Moncure, a spokeswoman for Frontex, which is based in Warsaw, said Tuesday that the agency had already deployed four coastal vessels and two aircraft this year as part of an operation to patrol the central Mediterranean route used by the migrants who died near Lampedusa.
Ms. Moncure said Frontex was waiting for the outcome of the meeting of justice and interior ministers in Luxembourg before issuing a statement about any additional patrol work in the Mediterranean. “But with such high numbers of migrants arriving,” she said, “we are definitely going to prolong our operation beyond October.”
Analysts warned, however, that allocating more resources to Frontex, which works primarily to coordinate member states’ border operations, would not automatically make the agency more effective.
“Frontex would be very enthusiastic for this kind of wider role, but there is a big question over how this would really work, given that Frontex does not have any direct operational powers,” saidJoanna Parkin, a migration specialist at the Center for European Policy Studies, in Brussels.
“A second serious question,” she said, “is whether Frontex should have this kind of enhanced role given the ongoing questions about whether Frontex can be held responsible for failures to protect migrants on the Mediterranean Sea or whether it can ensure that potential refugees are given proper protection when they reach Europe, rather than get pushed back.”
Many of the migrants on the central Mediterranean route originate from countries in the Horn of Africa and western Africa, and they use Libya as a nexus before starting their journey toward Europe, according to astatementthat Frontex posted on its Web site. Most of those migrants land on Lampedusa or Sicily, but others make landfall elsewhere in Italy, usually around Calabria and Puglia, the agency said.
During the first nine months of the year, more than 31,000 migrants arrived in the European Union using the central Mediterranean route, most of them Eritreans, Somalis and other sub-Saharan Africans, as well as Syrians, Frontex said. Migratory pressure this summer was comparable to that in the summer of 2011, when civil unrest in Tunisia and Libya led over the course of that year to about 60,000 arrivals on the central Mediterranean route, the agency said.
Many migrants make the journey aboard overcrowded wooden fishing boats that are prone to sinking or capsizing in high seas and are ill equipped, with poor engines and navigation systems.
“Often, the drivers of these boats leave them adrift relatively near the Italian shores and go back where they came from,” Renato Di Natale, the chief prosecutor in Agrigento, said by telephone.

To Shop: Another Salumeria for the Upper East Side
Fabio Casella (right), who comes from Salerno, Italy, shares a surname but no relatives with the better-known chef Cesare Casella, from Tuscany. Though Fabio Casella’s career path is more modest, like Cesare he now has a salumeria on the Upper East Side. Il Salumaio, his compact new storefront a few doors south of his pizzeria, San Matteo, specializes in hams, salamis, cheeses, condiments and groceries imported from Italy. “I can sell my 18-month-old San Pietro prosciutto di Parma for $16.99 a pound and still make a profit,” he said. “This is a neighborhood place and I don’t have to charge more.” A few pasta dishes are made fresh, along with sandwiches layered on magically delicate focaccia baked in the pizzeria’s wood-burning oven:
Il Salumaio, 1731 Second Avenue (90th Street), (646) 852-6876,
ilsalumaiony.com
.
Chester Higgins Jr./The New York Times
To Shred: An Unusual Vegetable in a Quick Summer Slaw
How often have you actually gone shopping for kohlrabi, those weird, cabbagelike spheres sprouting stems and leaves? If you belong to a community-supported agriculture group, you may have acquired some without even asking. Otherwise, consider buying some for a quick summer slaw. Take large kohlrabi, four to six ounces each, remove the stems, peel them and shred them in your food processor. Then toss with about a half-cup each of yogurt and mayonnaise sharpened with a splash of rice vinegar, salt and pepper. Letting the slaw sit for several hours improves it; the finishing touches are a few tablespoons of minced dill and a bunch of scallions, finely chopped.
Chester Higgins Jr./The New York Times
To Quench: A Wheat Beer Lavished With Belgian Undertones
To beer lovers, summer often means lip-smackingly refreshing wheat beer. The Brooklyn Brewery’s version, created in collaboration with GrowNYC, which runs the Greenmarkets, is a pale amber brew that conveys hints of orange zest and coriander, flavorings that are typical in Belgium. It is made from upstate wheat and Massachusetts barley malt, uses Belgian yeast and undergoes a natural second fermentation, like Champagne, in the bottle. The alcohol level is 5.5 percent:
Brooklyn Greenmarket Wheat is $10 for 750 milliliters at the Union Square Greenmarket on Wednesdays and Saturdays.
Chester Higgins Jr./The New York Times
To Fill: Ice Cream Cones for All Manner of Goodies
These ice cream cones in white ceramic — brightly rimmed in aqua, vermilion or yellow — are ideal for serving not just gelato, sorbet, ice cream or other frozen confections, but also berries and cream, puddings and even iced espresso. They’re dishwasher safe and house-gift perfect:
Anthropologie stores, $6 each, and
anthropologie.com
.
Tony Cenicola/The New York Times
To Flavor: Concentrated Shiitake in a Bottle
Call it liquid shiitake mushrooms. This dark, rich concentrate imported from Japan is ready to add to sauces or brush on meat, fish or vegetables, contributing a deep, mushroomy finish to the flavor. It’s also excellent to add to pasta, like a toss of cool soba with sautéed shiitakes. Use it sparingly; a little goes a long way:
Mitoku Concentrated Shiitake Broth is $15 for 10 ounces at Bklyn Larder, 228 Flatbush Avenue (Bergen Street), Park Slope, Brooklyn; (718) 783-1250,
bklynlarder.com
.
Tony Cenicola/The New York Times
To Read: From a Group of Chefs That Wastes Not
Twenty years ago in Hawaii, a handful of American chefs formed the Chefs Collaborative, a group that advocates for sustainable seafood, improving the quality of school lunches and preserving local farmland. As you might imagine, the group’s first cookbook is more than a collection of recipes. Beautifully photographed dishes like zucchini and Vidalia onion fritters, clams with sorrel and cream, and almond ricotta cake with roasted apricots are interspersed with pages of valuable information. Learn the labeling rules for eggs, poultry and meat; why organic ingredients cost more; and chefs’ tips on wasting nothing, like pumpkin stems in a recipe for pumpkin curry with potatoes, which I cannot wait to try come fall:
“The Chefs Collaborative Cookbook” by the Chefs Collaborative and Ellen Jackson (Taunton Press, $40)
.
Yana Paskova for The New York Times
To Indulge: One More Spot to Give In to Temptation
Lady M Confections is expanding its New York footprint, and with it, your waistline. A new patisserie and cafe, done in the company’s signature white minimalist style, features the elegant layered mille crepe cake, seasonal tarte aux fruits (above), Mont Blanc, strawberry shortcake and other alluring choices sold by the slice or the whole cake in two sizes. For the new store, which also offers some breakfast pastries, sandwiches and salads, there is a green tea éclair iced in white chocolate and matcha, and shimmering with sparkle dust:
Cakes are $40 to $80, 36 West 40th Street, (212) 452-2222,
ladym.com
.

Michael R. Bloomberg, dissatisfied with his potential successors and unsure his political blessing would help anyone, on Friday ruled out making an endorsement in the general election for New York City mayor.
Mayor Michael R. Bloomberg promised to assist whoever prevailed in the November general election.
Follow us onTwitterand like us onFacebookfor news and conversation.
The surprise decision ended weeks of speculation that Mayor Bloomberg might try to tip the scales in a campaign that has become, in many ways, a referendum on his 12-year legacy in office.
It was a curious turn for a man who relishes giving his stamp of approval to candidates from Rhode Island to California and who might never have become mayor without the endorsement of his own immediate predecessor, Rudolph W. Giuliani, in the days after the Sept. 11 attacks.
But Mr. Bloomberg and his aides, who conduct their own polls and study the public mood carefully, have concluded that the electorate is not looking to him for advice on the next occupant of City Hall, and they determined that his endorsement would offer little advantage to the recipient.
Mr. Bloomberg seemed to hint at that on Friday during his radio show when he declared: âI donât want to do anything that complicates it for the next mayor. And thatâs one of the reasons Iâve decided Iâm just not going to make an endorsement in the race.â
He vowed instead to devote his remaining days in office to governing and promised to assist whoever prevails in November. âI want to make sure that person is ready to succeed, to take what weâve done and build on that,â he said.
His armâs-length approach to the general election reflects an unhappy reality: the mayorâs favored candidate in the field, Christine C. Quinn, the City Council speaker, performed dismally in the Democratic primary, largely because of her close ties to him.
That, to say the least, has not improvedthe desirabilityof Mr. Bloombergâs endorsement this year.
At the same time, Mr. Bloomberg, a onetime Democrat who was elected mayor as a Republican and then became an independent, has little relationship with, or affection for, either the leading Democrat in the race, Bill de Blasio, or the Republican nominee, Joseph J. Lhota.
Mr. de Blasio, the public advocate, surged to the top of the field by hammering Mr. Bloombergâs policies and worldview, earning his ire; Mr. Lhotaonce called Mr. Bloomberg an âidiotâwithin earshot of a reporter, which has not exactly endeared him to the mayor.
Still, by all accounts, Mr. Bloomberg was intrigued by Mr. Lhota, a fellow businessman turned government official whose fiscal conservatism and social liberalism matched his own.
And Mr. Lhota has at various times implied he would seek Mr. Bloombergâs endorsement.
But by Friday, Mr. Lhota, a former deputy mayor under Mr. Giuliani, sounded as reluctant to accept a Bloomberg endorsement as the mayor was to grant it.
âMany people want to pigeonhole me as being an extension of Bloomberg, an extension of Mayor Giuliani,â he said in a brief appearance on WPIX-TV. âIâm actually not either of those two guys, and itâs quite obvious. And I want everybody to know whatâs different about me and what I would do differently than the prior mayors.â
Advisers to Mr. Lhota saw an upside in the mayoral snub: It will liberate him to speak critically about Mr. Bloomberg, a necessary tactic in a race in which, to win, he must appeal to Democrats, who vastly outnumber Republicans in the city.
Mr. de Blasio, speaking in Brooklyn, seemed downright thrilled by the mayorâs neutrality, calling it âan admirable and appropriate act.â Asked about his own slim prospects for a mayoral endorsement, he replied mischievously: âI was waiting by the phone.â
By opting not to weigh in on the race, aides said, Mr. Bloomberg is beginning a long-planned process of removing himself from the cityâs political sphere.
In private conversations with friends and colleagues, Mr. Bloomberg has said he does not want to hover over his successor or to be seen as meddlesome â a hanger-on, unable to give up power.
He has recalled, with fondness, Mr. Giulianiâs decision to leave the cityâs political stage after 2001 and to refrain from criticism of Mr. Bloombergâs performance as mayor.
âIt was the biggest gift Rudy gave me,â Mr. Bloomberg has said in these conversations.
Of course, that kind of discipline is easier to discuss than to deliver: Mr. Bloomberg has repeatedly engaged in pointed critiques of the mayoral candidates this year, at times to his own detriment, like whenhe described Mr. de Blasioâs campaign as âracistâfor featuring his biracial family.
Those close to Mr. Bloomberg said his announcement on Friday signified a new and serious attempt by the mayor to stop discussing the campaign and the candidates in it until after the election.
His goal until the end of his term: to project a bipartisan, statesmanlike air, and not become bogged down in city politics.
âIâve never been a partisan guy,â Mr. Bloomberg said on his radio show. âI think people have appreciated that over the years.â
âMy job,â he concluded, âis to govern.â
Michael M. Grynbaum and Thomas Kaplan contributed reporting.

Kate Buesser is practicing with the U.S. women’s hockey team while seeking admission to medical school. She hopes to be on the roster for Sochi.
LAKE PLACID, N.Y. — Her Olympic hopes seemingly extinguished, Kate Buesser’s idea of mucking it up in the corners involved a lobster boat, not hockey.
In April, Buesser had an offer to measure lobsters and bait pots on a boat in Maine. Her plan was to save some money, then take a trip to Europe before applying to medical school. She intends to become a surgeon.
Earlier that month, Buesser had written a letter to Katey Stone, the United States Olympic coach, who also coached her at Harvard. Her skills had improved and her mental approach had grown more ebullient, Buesser wrote, after playing for two seasons in the Canadian Women’s Hockey League.
In late April, Buesser received a phone call. She was invited to an Olympic tryout camp in June. Lobstering would have to wait.
“They were understanding,” Buesser, 24, said, laughing. “It’s easy for them to pick up a stern man.”
A forward, Buesser impressed at the June tryout and was named one of 25 players on the national team roster. The members of the Olympic roster, which must be trimmed to 21 players, will be named Jan. 1. The United States is the reigning world champion and is expected to challenge Canada, the defending Olympic champion, for gold in February at the Winter Games in Sochi, Russia.
Buesser is considered to be on the bubble. But she has persisted with speed and grittiness. Last Saturday, she scored her first goal for the national team in an 8-1 defeat of Sweden in the consolation game of the Four Nations Cup.
“She has just kept earning her way,” Stone said.
There is one catch. Hockey season and medical school application season run concurrently. On Monday night, Buesser is scheduled to fly across the country for a Tuesday interview at the School of Medicine at the University of California, San Francisco. She will then catch a redeye flight back to Boston for hockey practice on Wednesday.
Unlike N.H.L. players, who will fill the men’s rosters in Sochi, female players are hardly millionaires. Buesser said that until she became eligible for a training stipend from the United States Olympic Committee, she had never made a dime from the sport since graduating from Harvard in 2011.
Her teammates are in a similar situation. Some are living with host families in the Boston area to cut costs. Buesser lives with an aunt. Ten of the American players are students, including Jincy Dunne, 16, who has her assignments sent from her high school outside St. Louis.
“They’re pretty lenient,” Dunne said. “I think I’ll be doing a little summer school, but I have no complaints.”
Each player must find her own motivation beyond financial reward. Lyndsey Fry, a forward who is taking a year off from Harvard, is inspired in part by the memory of a friend, Liz Turgeon, the daughter of the former N.H.L. all-star Pierre Turgeon who was killed in an automobile accident in December 2010.
The two women played on a select team in Colorado during high school and promised to push each other to make an Olympic team. Fry now hangs Liz Turgeon’s jersey in her locker before each game.
“There have been times training for this that her memory has pushed me through,” Fry said. “I know she’s right there with me the whole way.”
Buesser was invited to a camp with the national team in August 2010, before her senior year at Harvard. But it was a solemn moment in her life. Her father had died three months earlier in a motorcycle accident.
“She wasn’t her best version of herself as a hockey player,” said Caitlin Cahow, a two-time Olympian who mentored Buesser at Harvard and is a close friend. “She was still emotionally raw. That kind of put her on the shelf with U.S. Hockey for a while.”
Buesser’s senior season at Harvard became a kind of chore, played amid personal loss and the responsibilities of being a team captain and completing her degree in organismic and evolutionary biology.
“I don’t think I was looking at hockey as a fun time,” Buesser said. “It was work.”

NEW DELHI — Indian troops have shot and killed another Pakistani soldier at the de facto border between the two countries inKashmir, adding to an unusually tense period in the disputed region.
Follow@nytimesworldfor international breaking news and headlines.
A statement released Friday by the Indian Army said that Indian soldiers saw an intruder at 3 p.m. on Thursday in the Nowshera sector of the so-called Line of Control separating the Indian- and Pakistani-held parts of Kashmir. The Indian soldiers challenged the intruder, who “opened indiscriminate fire,” wounding two soldiers, the statement said. The soldiers returned fire and later found a dead Pakistani soldier in uniform, it said.
On Friday at 10 a.m., Pakistani commanders called their Indian counterparts and asked for the body to be returned, according to the Indian statement.
“Acceding to this request,” the statement said, “the dead body was returned toPakistanArmy personnel in the same sector in the evening with military respects.”
A Pakistani military official sent a text message to reporters on Friday saying that the soldier had accidentally crossed the boundary, The Associated Press reported.
The A.P. report said the Pakistani military later issued a statement accusing the Indian troops of killing the soldier after he had explained his mistake.
“We condemn such an inhuman and brutal act of killing our soldier after he had identified himself and explained his position,” the statement said, according to The A. P.
Last month, three Pakistani soldiers andtwo Indian soldiers were killedat the border, and one of the Indian soldierswas beheaded. The killings heightened tensions between the two nuclear-armed countries to their worst since they agreed to a cease-fire in 2003. The two countries have been in conflict over Kashmir almost since their founding in 1947.
Border skirmishes are not the only immediate problem in Kashmir. Last weekend,Indian authorities executed Mohammed Afzal, known as Afzal Guru, a Kashmiri who was convicted of participating in a 2001 attack onIndia’s Parliament. Many in Kashmir believe that Mr. Afzal did not receive a fair trial, and have expressed outrage over the timing and circumstances of his execution.
Indian officials put a curfew in place following the execution. Nevertheless, as many as 25 protests have flared around the Indian-held parts of Kashmir, and there have been at least 110 arrests. The region is predominantly Muslim, and curfew was tightened Friday, the Muslim day of prayer, and many of the major mosques in Srinagar, the main city, were closed Friday.
The week before the execution, aKashmiri girls’ rock band decided to disbandafter threats against the members were posted on social media sites and a top Muslim cleric asked that they stop performing.

A New York City police officer and his two brothers, one of whom is also a law enforcement agent, smuggled military-grade firearms capable of piercing body armor and shooting down airplanes to the Philippines, prosecutors said on Friday.
Follow us onTwitterand like us onFacebookfor news and conversation.
Prosecutors said that the officer, Rex G. Maralit, joined his brothers, Wilfredo Maralit, a Customs and Border Protection officer at Los Angeles International Airport, and Ariel Maralit, who lives in the Philippines, in the smuggling scheme. The brothers arranged to send assault rifles, sniper rifles, pistols and weapons accessories abroad, skirting federal laws that prohibit the sale of weapons overseas without permission from the government and without licenses.
Court papers described Ariel Maralit, 43, as the point person in the Philippines, finding customers there who requested, for example, a .50-caliber long-range rifle or a high-capacity semiautomatic pistol. Officer Maralit, 44, and Wilfredo Maralit, 48, scoured gun Web sites for bargains, sometimes using their law enforcement credentials to obtain discounts. They then disassembled the weapons and shipped them disguised as other objects.
The men were charged with conspiring to violate the federal Arms Export Control Act by exporting the weapons without the permission of the government and conspiring to sell firearms without a license. They face up to five years in prison on each charge.
Officer Maralit made his first court appearance in the Federal District Court in Brooklyn on Friday, wearing a white polo-style shirt and dark slacks. He will be held in custody until at least next week, when his public defender, Michelle A. Gelernt, is expected to argue for his release on bail.
Wilfredo Maralit was expected to appear in federal court in California on Friday before being sent to Brooklyn to face the charges. Ariel Maralit is believed to be in the Philippines and has not yet been taken into custody.
The investigation began in February with a tip from a confidential informant in the Philippines. Investigators learned that the brothers participated in the scheme from January 2009 to March 2013, according to the criminal complaint.
In one instance in July 2009, Ariel Maralit sent an e-mail from the Philippines to his brothers with the subject line, âbuying orders.â The e-mail listed the proposed purchase prices, sale prices and the net profits that each brother would receive for several guns, including a pistol that was designed as a battlefield weapon, according to the complaint. The e-mail instructed the brothers to purchase the weapons âasap and send before October.â
Prosecutors said there was no evidence to indicate that the smuggled weapons were provided to criminal gangs, kidnappers or terrorist organizations.
Photographic exhibits filed in the case show the men holding rifles as big as their torsos and smiling. Prosecutors filed the photographs as part of their argument that the men were too dangerous to be released on bail.
âRather than upholding and enforcing the law as they had sworn to do, these defendants made international gunrunning a family business,â Loretta E. Lynch, the United States attorney for the Eastern District, said in a statement. âThe brothers used their knowledge of the law to circumvent it.â

Gennady Golovkin will perform twice on Broadway this week: once inside a theater, and once inside a boxing ring.
Mr. Golovkin, a middleweight champion who grew up in Kazakhstan and now lives in Germany, will make his acting debut in “Rocky,” the musical, at the Winter Garden Theater on Wednesday evening.
On Saturday night, he will try to defend his two world titles at Madison Square Garden.
He is hoping that the unusual double feature draws rave reviews.
“I would like both audiences to appreciate my performances,” Mr. Golovkin, 32, said through an interpreter while walking in Manhattan on Monday. “I plan on being as earnest in the ring as I am on stage, and I hope people will see that.”
After making his way to the theater where he will perform, as the fighter who inherits Rocky Balboa’s old gym locker, Mr. Golovkin said he was “not really nervous about either event.”
“I’m more excited than nervous about both of these opportunities,” he said with a confident smile.
“I can handle myself in the ring, and even though I have no acting experience, maybe this is the start of a new career,” he said. “Maybe I can become the next James Bond.”
Mr. Golovkin, who has an undefeated record of 29-0 with 26 knockouts, is hoping that his four-year title reign lasts beyond “Rocky,” which opened in March and is scheduled to close on Aug. 17. When the curtain drops for the final time, the show will have run for 28 previews and just 188 regular performances.
“Anytime a curtain closes forever,” Mr. Golovkin said, “that’s always sad news.” Still, Mr. Golovkin appreciates the opportunity, which occurred by chance. The producers wanted a true boxer to be in the show, and when they learned that Mr. Golovkin would be in town this week, they offered him the part. No audition necessary.
Mr. Golovkin, who has never been to a Broadway show, went to the Winter Garden Theater on Monday to meet Andy Karl, the actor
who plays Rocky Balboa
.
“It’s good to finally meet my co-star,” Mr. Golovkin said to Mr. Karl, as both men laughed.
They hugged and chatted briefly beneath the theater’s marquee, attracting a crowd of curious onlookers, many of whom began snapping photos with their cellphones. Mr. Karl then asked Mr. Golovkin to autograph a boxing glove he brought along.
“The idea of having the middleweight champion of the world on stage with me is kind of blowing my mind,” Mr. Karl said, staring at Mr. Golovkin’s signature as he spoke. “I’m a boxing fan who has been training every day like a real boxer for the show, so this is a very special occasion for me. I’m excited, and so is the rest of the cast.”
Mr. Karl, who will return the favor by rooting for Mr. Golovkin at Madison Square Garden on Saturday night, said jokingly that “if Gennady can deal with the pressures of acting before a Broadway audience, he will be fine in the ring.”
“My money is on Gennady, both here and at the Garden,” Mr. Karl said. “Look, he’s the guy who is getting Rocky’s old locker. That has to tell you that he is considered a rising star.”
Continue reading the main story
Continue reading the main story
Mr. Golovkin, who holds both the World Boxing Association and International Boxing Organization titles, said he was thrilled to have even a bit part in the stage adaptation of the Rocky saga. The original “Rocky” movie came out in 1976, and was followed by several sequels. He said the story of the underdog boxer and his rise to fame played a significant role in guiding him on the path to a professional boxing career.
“When I was a young boy, those Rocky movies were my favorite, especially the wild and exciting endings,” he said. “I loved those films; they inspired me to want to become a fighter. Even today, I still watch them for inspiration.”
Just as Rocky’s family and friends in Philadelphia cheered for him in the movies, so too will Golovkin’s real-life entourage be cheering for him in his native Kazakhstan, as well as in Stuttgart, Germany, where he lives with his wife and young son, and in Big Bear, Calif., where he trains.
“This is one of the most important weeks of my life, as I have to pass two important tests,” Mr. Golovkin said. “I’m a performer, that’s what I do, and I want to make everyone proud of my two performances on Broadway.”
Mr. Golovkin knows that he will fare best this week if he can create drama on Wednesday, while avoiding it during Saturday’s 12-round bout against Daniel Geale, which will be shown on HBO.
“Those Rocky endings are great in the movies and on the stage,” he said, “but I don’t think it would be too smart for me to get into that kind of fight in the ring.”

This morning dawns under the long shadow of the death of the designer Oscar de la Renta, whose absence will be felt deeply in American fashion. In his memory, I thought it worth mentioning two events from Monday that Mr. de la Renta, of all people, would probably have appreciated.
To be specific: The sudden embrace of high-end fashion by
Mayor Bill de Blasio
of New York and President François Hollande of France, politicians who have had uneasy relationships with the luxury world.
Mr. de la Renta understood the value of working with the political establishment in a wholly bipartisan way. He was, in fact, a master of the form, going back as far as the administration of John F. Kennedy and spanning the Reagan-Bush-Clinton-Bush-Obama (
finally
) White Houses.
So he probably would have applauded the decision by Mr. de Blasio to declare this week “Madison Avenue Fashion Heritage Week.”
Calling Madison Avenue “the fashion epicenter of the five boroughs,” Mr. de Blasio said, “This week, we will celebrate 16 of these Madison Avenue-based fashion brands that have not only found success in our city and beyond, but have also maintained their position as leaders in the industry for decades.”
(They have presumably also paid a lot in rent and taxes.)
The brands include Dolce & Gabbana, Emilio Pucci, Ermenegildo Zegna, Hermès and Akris, among others.
Also on Monday, albeit across the Atlantic, Mr. Hollande officially opened
the Fondation Louis Vuitton
, a contemporary art museum and performance space in Paris. He was seen talking and posing happily with Bernard Arnault, the chairman of LVMH Moët Hennessy Louis Vuitton and a friend of Nicolas Sarkozy, a former French president and Mr. Hollande’s rival.
Mr. Hollande did not stay for the inaugural dinner (soup with white truffles, veal cheeks with chanterelles, caramelized pears and crème brûlée), but Fleur Pellerin, the minister of culture and communication, and Anne Hidalgo, the mayor of Paris, represented the national and city governments. Also in attendance were LVMH designers such as Karl Lagerfeld (Fendi) and Nicolas Ghesquière (Louis Vuitton), members of the Arnault family, and celebrities like the actress Michelle Williams and the director Sofia Coppola.
The message in both instances was pretty clear: Fashion for the seemingly 1 percent, as an industry and as a benefactor, matters. These days, politicians — even the ones that run on more populous platforms — cannot afford to look away.
Related:
Oscar de la Renta, Who Clothed Stars and Became One, Dies at 82

CANTON, Ohio — Football has long since overtaken baseball as the United States’ most popular sport. But when it comes to pomp and nostalgia, baseball has been the leader thanks in part to the National Baseball Hall of Fame and Museum in Cooperstown, N.Y.
In the last decade, though, the Pro Football Hall of Fame has worked to burnish its legacy by revamping its museum and programs to prepare for the centennial of the founding of what became the N.F.L.
Last year, the museum completed a $28 million renovation that enhanced its archives, reconfigured displays and expanded its event space and gift shop. The museum also started taking part of its collection
on the road
, traveling to cities like New Orleans, Pittsburgh and St. Louis for three months at a time. For the first time, the Hall hosted a fan festival, in Cleveland, attracting about 20,000 visitors.
The improvements in Canton and the traveling exhibits helped the Hall reach more fans, including 700,000 on the road. In 2013, 208,191 people visited the Hall, an increase of 11.6 percent compared with 2012 and the highest total since 1996. This in turn has increased the Hall’s revenue.
The Pro Football Hall of Fame generated $13 million in 2012, the last year figures were reported, compared with $8.3 million in revenue at the Baseball Hall of Fame. David Baker, who became president of the football Hall this year, expects revenue this year to jump as high as $20 million, about five times that of a decade ago.
“The brand is enormous, but it has been tremendously undervalued,” he said.
The higher profile of the football Hall is no accident. Last year, it celebrated its 50th anniversary. In 2020, the league — founded in Canton as the American Professional Football Association — will turn 100. At a time when the N.F.L. faces profound conflicts, including class-action lawsuits by retirees over concussions, painkillers and the use of intellectual property, as well as serious questions about the cognitive health of its players, the Hall provides an emotional haven for fans.
Continue reading the main story
The league has long relied on NFL Films to document the history of its players, games and teams. NFL Network and ESPN, which last week agreed to cover the induction ceremony for seven more years, have provided more immediate documentation.
The Hall, a private nonprofit organization independent of the league, has tiptoed around controversial topics like head injuries and steroids, but it creates an image of the league that is less promotional and perhaps more credible than that offered by the N.F.L. and its affiliates.
“Football has historically suffered what you might call a romance deficit,” said Michael MacCambridge, who wrote “America’s Game: The Epic Story of How Pro Football Captured a Nation.” “It’s not that there isn’t a great amount of lore in football, but it was less part of the culture. They have to have that authenticity.”
The Hall has benefited significantly from a renewed interest by the N.F.L., its largest donor. In recent years, the league has worked closely with the Hall to put on the induction ceremony and the Hall of Fame Game — the Giants and the Buffalo Bills will play Sunday — and integrated the Hall of Fame into the league’s activities by, among other things, introducing many of the soon-to-be inductees at the draft. The league also moved its rookie symposium to Ohio in part so every incoming player could visit the Hall and be introduced to the history of the league.
Continue reading the main story
Continue reading the main story
“We have a responsibility and desire to tell the story of the past,” said Mark Waller, the chief marketing officer for the N.F.L. “What we have to do is a better job of integrating all these stories.”
The Hall produces just a sliver of the revenue created by the league, which has grown into a $10-billion-a-year business. But the growing prominence of the Hall speaks to the N.F.L.’s dominance on the sports landscape.
Part of league’s efforts to burnish its business is to solidify its history. Ralph Wilson Jr., the owner of the Bills until his death in March, donated $2.5 million during the renovation so the museum could upgrade its archives and safely store its 25 million pages of documents, nearly four million images, and countless jerseys, helmets and other gear.
In the museum’s new research and preservation center, Jon Kendle, a researcher, carefully opened a rule book from 1871, then showed off the museum’s book collection, which includes one of the first written about the professional game, “Pro Football: Its ‘Ups’ and ‘Downs,’ ” published in 1934 and written by the football executive and historian Dr. Harry March. (Oddly, the book stood next to “Football for Dummies” on the shelf.)
The documents, photos, magazines and books are kept in temperature-controlled rooms that are visible to visitors through glass walls.
“For years and years, we were downstairs in the basement, but we wanted to show it off,” Kendle said of the collection.
About 5 percent of the Hall’s collection is on view at any time. Most items are kept in storage, including about 800 footballs, 700 jerseys and 300 helmets. There are also games, cereal boxes, Gatorade buckets, bass drums from marching bands, film boxes and a desk used by Bert Bell, a former commissioner, complete with cigar burns.
“People think it’s just a bunch of guys running on the field, but it’s mind-boggling the level of effort people put into running the league,” said Jason Aikens, a curator.
Kendle and Aikens are now working to sift through and archive collections held by N.F.L. teams, starting with the Bills. Ultimately, the Hall would like to get into the business of curating team collections. But, as with its own artifacts, the museum has to be selective about what it stores because everything has a cost.
“Space is money,” Kendle said.
Indeed, as with the N.F.L. itself, bringing in more money is the ultimate goal. The Hall has vastly expanded its gift shop and increased the sale of its branded merchandise online, and in sporting goods and department stores, to reach fans nationwide.
“There are so many opportunities outside our four walls,” said Steve Strawbridge, who runs merchandise and licensing at the Hall.
Last week, the Hall also announced a multiyear partnership with 16W Marketing, a New Jersey-based company that will help find sponsors and marketing opportunities.
The Hall also plans to spend $25 million to overhaul Fawcett Stadium, next to the museum, and may redesign the entire campus.
“I don’t look at us as being in the Hall of Fame business; I look at us as a sports business,” said George Veras, the chief revenue officer and executive producer of the Hall. “That’s where you get the dollars.”

The writer pays homage to her childhood home — the hub of her family’s creativity and a refuge in a changing Greenwich Village.
An extraordinary transformation is underway on the block where I grew up — on 11th Street between Sixth and Seventh Avenues or, until recently, between Ray’s Pizza and St. Vincent’s Hospital. In a Manhattan district loved for its cobblestone mews and skewed streets, its human scale and warm brick facades, the former hospital — and future megacomplex of five new apartment buildings and five new townhouses — is now a vast 2.5-acre construction site, covered with enough roaring and shiny equipment to delight every small boy in the tristate area. Meanwhile, just down the block, the yellow house where my mother has lived since 1958 is a quiet and enduring oasis — one that has provided uninterrupted sanctuary to working artists for more than a century.
The writer Isabel Fonseca pays homage to her childhood home.
It came by chance, this long reign. Sixty years ago, my New Yorker mother went to Rome to study painting with the Italian artist Afro. He met her at the Spanish Steps with a huge bunch of red carnations and an apology: He’d been offered a show in America and was leaving the next day. Betty took her red flowers and her disappointment to the nearby Greco to get lunch, and to meet her future husband and the father of her four children. Gonzalo Fonseca, already an established artist and I guess hard to ignore with his black hair, tanned skin and white shirt, occupied the table with the only free seat in the restaurant. Rome, Paris, Tangier, Madrid — they moved around before settling, for the birth of my sister, Quina, in Montevideo, Uruguay.
Back in New York, my mother’s father was not happy. How to lure home his daughter and her sudden family? The answer became clear when he found the house on West 11th Street — the one with the irresistible artist’s studio, a silent, skylit painter’s paradise soaring upward in 40-feet twin peaks, built 60 feet out on what would have been the backyard. The house came with sitting tenants, but they would provide an income for the young artists. So sure was he of his plan that he bought the place without my parents having seen it, as a wedding present.
My parents were not the first artists to be enchanted by the studio — and the house’s four, then five, then six floors of living space, the structure having grown to fit our expanding family. Before the Fonsecas, it belonged to the modern dancer
Valerie Bettis
, who choreographed routines for Rita Hayworth. Long before that, the building was owned by
Daniel Chester French
, the American sculptor best known for his giant seated Lincoln, carved from 28 blocks of Georgia marble and assembled in Washington D.C. at the Lincoln Memorial. It was French who, in the late 1880s, added the studio.
The house has always kept its own rhythm. Gonzalo was Uruguayan and liked his supper late, often in the company of other artists, many of them European or South American, and so our mother routinely cooked two dinners. My siblings and I didn’t eat with the adults until we were about 12 years old, but we were expected to come down and say hello to guests, and, if they were foreigners, to kiss them on the cheek. We American kids were agonized by this, but our mother saved us some embarrassment by signaling the regionally correct number of pecks with one, two or sometimes three fingers pressed to her cheek. Apparently we met Jorge Luis Borges and Marcel Duchamp (who lived on 10th Street) — not that I cared. We were far more impressed when Hans Christian Andersen appeared at the table one night, in the form of Danny Kaye.
Painting united Elizabeth and Gonzalo who, in the beginning, shared no common language. Within five years, they had four children and, inevitably, my mother painted less. In the 1970s, Gonzalo turned to sculpture and for the rest of his life my brothers helped him haul back to the studio the hunks of marble, limestone and brownstone he’d find on demolition sites, those rich urban quarries. In time, my siblings joined the family trade. Quina became a costume designer, then a milliner and, always, a maker of exquisite things. Bruno and Caio, the brothers between us, both grew up to be painters.
Ours was a house divided, the adults below and the children upstairs. Sometimes it seemed we barely saw our father, who was as nocturnal as a western woolly lemur. Once I brought a friend home after school — and never again. We walked into the kitchen just as he was tucking into a boiled-egg breakfast, wild haired and in his bathrobe, at 3 in the afternoon. His studio on the ground floor was his sanctum sanctorum, a separate country in a different time zone, as it seemed to us, guarded by a forbidding sentry in a glass case behind my father’s desk in the studio’s marginally less dusty anteroom. “Uncle George,” as we called him, was a mummy from Papua New Guinea with shells for eyes and an apple-size shrunken head. Unlike my own children, who wander freely into both their parents’ studies, we wouldn’t have dared pass Uncle George.
On the sunny top floors, we exchanged our own currency: marble and wood building blocks, made for us by Gonzalo. Bruno was always at work on a tower, his goal to build an upside-down Empire State Building, starting with the spire. Caio, solid citizen then and now, built ramparts and fortresses. All I ever did with blocks was lay out the perimeters of apartments, within which I’d enact elaborate domestic dramas with my dolls (practice, perhaps, for my future as a fiction writer). Elizabeth was the kind of mother who came to school and did batik with the whole class but, at home, this business of “making art” was not organized or taught. Art was just what you did, mostly on your own.
As an adolescent, Bruno shut himself in his room and taught himself to draw, endlessly copying from huge books on Cézanne, Leonardo and Tiepolo. Across the hall, Caio also drew, but his work was topical, and funny. Undaunted by blanket rejection, he submitted hundreds of political cartoons to The New Yorker, sharpening his skills as our self-appointed debriefer during the unfolding Watergate scandal, monitoring the hearings on our tiny black-and-white TV and giving us daily updates, complete with voices. Quina made clothes, unfurling her bolts of fabric over the dining table our father had made from railroad timbers. And at the top of the house, in the small section of the attic that was standing height, I wrote and illustrated my little stories. Even with the clank and hiss of old radiators and the hum of hospital generators, that attic bedroom was the most peaceful place in the house, too hard for others to get to, the last stop on the line.
If the house was a refuge, the world outside was full of excitement and uncertainty. On 11th Street, my friends and I would jump rope for hours until the last call for dinner, while the boys played handball against the smooth white brick of P.S. 41. None of us will ever forget the deafening explosion — it seemed to detonate under our feet — one Friday in the spring of 1970, when the Weathermen accidentally blew themselves up on the next block. We raced down to have a look. There was a gap, crumbled and blackened like a rotten tooth, in the row of Greek Revival townhouses and, through a hole torn into the next house, a table (apparently the desk of Dustin Hoffman, who lived there), artistically pitched over the rubble.
The radical tone of the neighborhood was set by our neighbors, neo-Luddite Kirkpatrick Sale and the great short-story writers
Grace Paley
and
Donald Barthelme
. Paley was the queen of street resistance, as well as the friendly face, framed by electrified gray hair, that presided from a high window in the apartment building across the way. We were happy to interrupt our games to cheer on the protests — “Less rent, more control!” and “Free Soviet Jewry!” — especially as we thought we’d be showered with free Soviet jewelry. Caio and I, at 12 and 10, proudly joined Grace on marches down to Washington Square, once to “Free Angela!” from the Women’s House of Detention on Greenwich Avenue, and another time in a protest against Governor Nelson Rockefeller’s handling of a prison riot upstate. “Attica! Attica!” we chanted. I was very surprised, a few years later, when I saw “Dog Day Afternoon.” How had Al Pacino known about that? That was our chant.
Although the city in the 1970s was wilder and more dangerous than it is now — at some point everyone got flashed or mugged or hassled by drug dealers — children were infinitely freer. We saw a lot and understood little — about, for example, the mustachioed leather-clad men and fully feathered drag queens who catwalked down Seventh Avenue. The Stonewall riots had happened only a few blocks away, but we didn’t know you could be fired, or arrested, for being gay; we didn’t grasp that pageant was protest. To us, the riveting, sometimes frightening street scene only enhanced the appeal of the safer, work-driven ethos of home. Our parents were liberals but they were not activists. They were artists — people who, by one definition, are most alive when alone.
After our parents split and our father left the studio for good, the block received another maverick writer in
Richard Cornuelle
, our new stepfather, and our tentatively liberated mother finally created a proper studio of her own. Instead of taking over the entire ground-floor space, she installed a high-walled mezzanine at the back of the big room — a loft that, to this day, is accessed only by her via a ladder and through a trap door. It was a revelation, like our own Berlin Wall coming down, when our mother and Dick connected the studio to the house with a new stairway from the kitchen and cleared the space for parties. At one packed soiree, Warren Beatty suavely asked me, slightly flustered in a little black dress, why all the girls in L.A. wore white while the girls of New York stuck with black.
Freedom to experiment, the essential condition of any artist’s studio, continued to infuse the space. In the disco era, we hung our Christmas tree from the upper reaches of the ceiling, its lights dazzling like a mirror ball as it worryingly spun. Another year, my mother, disinclined to go out in the snow and buy a tree, just painted one onto the wall.
There have been period revamps, beginning in the 1950s with the stripping out of all moldings and other curly plaster fripperies. The smoky-brown mirrors of the 1970s have in their turn been evicted, but the track lighting, the burlap walls and the 50-year-old Magic Chef remain. In the 1990s, my sister Quina and her then-husband, the architect Jonathan Marvel, raised their children in the house and added another floor, right over my old attic — a new studio with a sunlit terrace looking out over the Jefferson Market clock tower. But as the parties wound down and the rest of us moved out, the essential setting remained intact — every chair, object, textile and child’s lumpy clay creation secure in its appointed spot.
Our parents amassed treasures on their travels together — to Europe, to the junk shops on Bleecker Street and the auction houses on Second Avenue, and also, for some long months without us, down the Orinoco River — and on their travels alone. Around the time Helen Reddy made an appearance on the turntable, our newly single mother went to Tibet, and returned dragging rugs and decked in beads of turquoise and coral, with which she also festooned her four teenagers. All these things, whether made or bought or found or bargained for, do not constitute a formal collection. Just as the kitchen lamp is held together by a spoon, paintings are sometimes fastened to the wall with thumbtacks and framed only in duct tape. No professional decorator’s flourish here — instead, there was the simple example that objects you really care about, and acquire with an educated eye, can earn a lasting place. If our fond neighbors in the Village inspired us to march for change in the world, the house showed us what made the world worth saving.

Sylvia Saye held a basket of multicolored eggs.
“We won’t need to color them this year,” she said, looking at the eggs, which were already a pretty mix of blonde, tan, brown and light blue — and fresh from the henhouse on the side of her home in Queens.
Keeping chickens in New York City has become a popular hobby, especially in precincts of Brooklyn where foodies and do-it-yourselfers prize locally grown food. Ms. Saye, 48, bought a dozen heritage chickens last July to provide free-range eggs for her daughter, Scarlett, 5, because she wants to serve foods that are free of hormones.
“I got them for nutritional reasons,” Ms. Saye said. “You can’t buy these eggs in a supermarket.”
Ms. Saye bought a $2,500 coop and had fencing installed to protect the chickens from predators. But a month ago, she learned of a different type of threat to her chickens: the stringent restrictions that homeowners in her neighborhood are supposed to abide by.
Ms. Saye lives in Forest Hills Gardens, a private neighborhood nestled in one of the more pristine sections of New York City. It is renowned for its stately country garden style, multimillion-dollar Tudor and Georgian homes, and for its strict regulations, which forbid the keeping of backyard chickens.
Ms. Saye has been ordered by the Forest Hills Gardens Corporation, which manages the neighborhood, to get rid of the chickens. In a recent letter, corporation officials cited the nuisances section of a century-old homeowners’ covenant.
The rules were set down in 1913, during the infancy of this 140-acre, leafy swath that is one of America’s oldest planned communities and was designed by Frederick Law Olmsted, Jr., whose father was Frederick Law Olmsted, the famous landscape architect who helped design Central Park.
Ms. Saye called the nuisances section — which forbids “any brewery, distillery, malt house, slaughter house, brass foundry, tin, nail or other iron foundry, lime kiln or sugar bakery, tallow candlery, crematory, hospital, asylum” — antiquated.
She said she felt the rules should be modernized to fit the more progressive approach to raising poultry today, especially the heritage breeds that she owns.
She noted that the nuisances section also does not allow a “cattle yard, hog pen, fowl yard or house, cesspool, privy vault; nor any cattle, hogs or other live stock or live poultry.”
“These are 101-year-old rules,” she said, adding that she decided to order the chickens after seeing Martha Stewart talk about them on television. She even bought the birds from the website that Ms. Stewart recommended,
Mypetchicken.com
.
Mitchell Cohen, the president of the Forest Hills Gardens Corporation, said the regulations detailed in the homeowners’ covenant and restrictions are meant “to protect the whole community” and an “amazing document that has held the test of time.”
The covenant “is what has kept the Forest Hills Gardens the oasis it is today,” he said. “I’m sorry Mrs. Saye is upset by our letter, but to keep the Forest Hills Gardens the community it is, everyone must look beyond themselves and follow the rules we all agreed to follow.” The corporation has not said what, if any, action it might take to force Ms. Saye to evict her chickens. For now, she said, her chickens are staying.
Continue reading the main story
Continue reading the main story
The collision of a modern trend with the old laws has set tongues clucking in this staid neighborhood, but Ms. Saye said she has heard nothing but positive feedback from neighbors and no complaints about any odor, noise or unsightliness. They also praise her willingness to give away eggs, she said, as she sat in the sprawling Tudor house where she grew up and still lives on Greenway North, a scenic, winding way through this neighborhood, which is privately owned but open to traffic.
She said she bought the chickens to help keep a hormone-free diet for Scarlett, who she feared was developing faster than her peers. Ms. Saye wondered if the hormones in commercially-bought food could be affecting her daughter.
Ms. Saye bought a dozen young fancy chickens and ordered the Amish-built wooden chicken coop. She now has eight chickens, having given away two roosters and two chickens, which were creating too much of a racket.
This is no Old McDonald operation. Ms. Saye feeds the chickens an organic soy-free feed and a smorgasbord of fresh vegetables she buys from a nearby grocery store.
Looking at the flock, Ms. Saye pointed out a bantam Easter egger, a breed that lays blue eggs, and the black copper marans, a breed that is appreciated especially by French chefs for their eggs. The fluffy breeds were silkies, she said. Buffy, a cream-colored bird that Scarlett was hugging, was a Belgian bearded d’uccle.
The flock can produce a couple of dozen eggs per week, and it is Scarlett’s job each day to collect them, for breakfast or for baking.
One neighbor, Peter Vairo, whose backyard nearly abuts the chicken coop, said he had no problem with the flock.
“They’re quiet and not really noticeable,” he said, “and she’s keeping them for healthy living, not for slaughter.”

KEN JOHNSON

Postmodernism may mean different things to different people, but certainly one of its most memorable manifestations was that of the
Memphis Group
, the wildly influential, much-debated Milan-based designer collective started by
Ettore Sottsass
in 1981. Featuring playfully eccentric bookcases, tables, chairs, vases, lamps and other domestic items mostly by Mr. Sottsass and including works by six other group members, this exhilarating pair of exhibitions is a must for students of 20th-century design.
Reacting to Modernism’s law that form follows function, Memphis designers embraced a shamelessly hedonistic, anti-rational aesthetic. They used wood-grain-patterned laminates, molded plastics, fiberglass and bright, confectionery colors along with natural materials like wood and marble. They whimsically combined styles, patterns and motifs from earlier times: ancient Egyptian and Greek architecture, Art Deco, Bauhaus and California Funk. With their confectionery colors and eccentric shapes, Memphis works — now stars at auction — are like cartoon versions of furniture.
Among nearly three dozen pieces at
Koenig & Clinton
, Mr. Sottsass’s 1981 sideboard called “Casablanca” is emblematic of the Memphis spirit. Thick shelves stick out at different angles like blocky wings from the sides of a central vertical cabinet. Covered in red, yellow and black-and-white imitation-granite laminate, it resembles an exuberant robot. Along with other pieces by Mr. Sottsass at
Joe Sheftel
are elegant, oddly proportioned roll-top desks by Shiro Kuramata from 1980 and ’81. “Antibes” (1981) by George Sowden is a wooden cabinet elevated on slender legs lacquered in vibrant primary colors with a fractured crystalline pattern on its sides. High-minded critics reviled Memphis design in its time. Today its anti-puritanical ebullience is delightfully refreshing.
The president discussed the bipartisan bill that helps expand opportunity for more workers by ensuring they will have access to the training and skills they need to compete in the work force.

This week on the music charts, Maroon 5 opens with the No. 1 album and Meghan Trainor’s ode to a healthy-size derriere is the new No. 1 single.
Maroon 5’s latest, “V” (222/Interscope), sold 164,000 copies in its first week out, according to Nielsen SoundScan, taking the veteran pop-rock band to No. 1 for the second time in its career. The rapper Jeezy — you last knew him as Young Jeezy — is No. 2 with his new album, “Seen It All: The Autobiography” (Def Jam), which sold 121,000. Disney’s “Guardians of the Galaxy” soundtrack is No. 3 with 53,000, and Ariana Grande’s “My Everything” (Republic), last week’s No. 1, fell three spots to No. 4 with 42,000 sales, a 75 percent drop.
The biggest music news of this week was U2’s
surprise release
of its new album, “Songs of Innocence,” through a deal with Apple that instantly delivered the album free to what the company said were up to 500 million iTunes customers around the world.
As impressive as this method of release is, however, as far as Billboard and SoundScan are concerned it does not count as sales — so as a result “Songs of Innocence” will
not appear
on Billboard’s charts until after the album is commercially released, on Oct. 14.
Billboard has changed its chart policies various times over the years as the music industry has devised clever ways to generate insta-hits. In 2004, for example, after Prince gave away copies of his album “Musicology” with concert tickets, the music industry bible
ruled
that such giveaways would count only if consumers were given a choice about receiving the music. And a few years ago, after Amazon sold Lady Gaga’s album “Born This Way” for 99 cents, a rule was introduced excluding albums priced
under $3.49
in their first month of release.
U2’s new album also
cannot win a Grammy Award
next year. The eligibility period for the Grammys ends Sept. 30, and that window applies only to the full commercial release of a record, not any advance promotional appearance, Billboard reported.
Also on the chart this week, week, Bob Marley’s “Legend” (Island), a hits collection from 1984 that is one of the best-selling catalog albums ever, reached the Top 10 for the first time. It rose 95 spots to No. 5 this week with 41,000 sales, helped by a 99-cent promotion on Google’s Play store. (The week before, “Legend” sold just over 3,000 copies.) And Counting Crows’ new album, “Somewhere Under Wonderland” (Capitol), opened at No. 6 with 32,000 sales.
On the singles chart, after a two-week reign by Taylor Swift’s “Shake It Off,” Ms. Trainor’s “All About That Bass” reaches No. 1. A positive-body-image dance hit about having “all the right junk in all the right places,” the song had 312,000 downloads and 13.1 million streams.
