“Katie is smart, attractive, fun and she knows all the words to ‘Live Like You Were Dying,’ ” said Brian Sumers as he described Katie Murphy, his girlfriend of three years.
The lyrics of that Tim McGraw song serve as a poignant reminder of one of the couple’s early dates — one that nearly ended in tragedy and yet cemented their commitment to each other.
Then I loved deeper and I spoke sweeter
And I watched an eagle as it was flying
And he said someday I hope you get the chance
To live like you were dying.
The couple had been dating a few months when in July 2011, Mr. Sumers, 31, a staff correspondent in Los Angeles for Aviation Week magazine whom Ms. Murphy describes as “someone who gets itchy if he’s not on a plane to somewhere each week,” arranged a short getaway to a dude ranch near the Grand Tetons in Wyoming. A teenage ranch hand invited the couple along for a staff rafting trip.
The rafts were loaded with beer, but not life jackets, as they entered a tributary of the Snake River, which, with melting snow, was then running high and fast. When the raft they were riding in hit a dam and flipped, the undertow and currents kept them underwater for what “felt like forever,” Mr. Sumers said.
The near drowning of the two ended with each clambering to the river’s bank, battered and bloodied. After hours of hiking, the couple reached the run-down cabin of an eccentric mountain man, who gave them a ride back to the ranch.
There, over dinner, Ms. Murphy, a 30-year-old executive with Smokehouse Pictures, a Hollywood production company owned in part by the actor George Clooney, heard Mr. Sumers first refer to her as his “girlfriend.” The sound of that word pierced through her exhaustion. It also set off a flood of emotion and a pressing question.
“I’m your girlfriend?” she asked.
The answer from Mr. Sumers was an unequivocal “Yes.”
By the conclusion of their Wyoming trip, near drowning and all, the couple talked about a trust and bond they now knew existed between them. Ms. Murphy said, “We got closer faster” than any couple they had ever known. By 2012, the couple’s relationship had progressed so rapidly that they were living together.
Continue reading the main story
They are also so like-minded that their conversations are peppered with references to the lingo of their industries, like “the elements of story,” “narrative” and “character.”
Back in April 2011, the two had met through Match.com, the online dating site. His profile photo, taken of him at a rodeo, intrigued her. It was Ms. Murphy’s first Match.com date. Before heading to meet him at the Woods, an urbane Hollywood bar outfitted with tables carved from tree trunks and chandeliers made from elk horns, she posted a selfie and described her outfit. On Instagram, however, she declared that her expectations were low, friends said.
A Match.com date for Mr. Sumers had an arc — one hour and one drink — and a name, “Date Zero.” Over five years, he had gone on 50 boring online dates. But he knew this one was working. Three drinks and three hours later, the two were still trading clever quips and laughter, and the time flew.
Continue reading the main story
“There was an immediate spark and sarcastic repartee,” Ms. Murphy said, “and Brian really brought it.”
Mr. Sumers knew he wanted to see more of Ms. Murphy. They met once more, an all-day date, before he left Los Angeles for a fellowship in Germany.
“I was so smitten that it was the longest four weeks of my life,” she said. “It was so fresh and exciting.”
After he returned to Los Angeles, subsequent dates revealed more uncanny shared interests: their passion for order, travel and food, including the “never-ending chips and salsa” at Chili’s Bar & Grill, Ms. Murphy said. While driving home in her car, she heard one of Mr. Sumers’s favorite country songs come on the radio. Both knew the lyrics by heart, and it turned out that both were “Tim McGraw super fans,” Mr. Sumers said. “You wouldn’t think so, because I’m from Chicago and New York and live in L.A.”
In Marietta, Ga., where Ms. Murphy grew up, Mr. McGraw’s music had seeped into her subconscious from the jukebox at the LongHorn Steakhouse, where she worked in high school. So did a love of the arts, and specifically film.
Ms. Murphy, who has assisted at Smokehouse Pictures with “The Ides of March,” “The Men Who Stare at Goats” and “Argo,” grew up in a “culture of creativity,” said Ben Mayer, a childhood friend from Marietta. “And Katie was always at the center of that.”
At Sope Creek Elementary School, he and Ms. Murphy produced a nine-minute video, ostensibly as a third-grade science project, he said, but it ended “with six minutes of credits, all puns.” Other childhood projects included “Geriatric Park,” a satire about Marietta; music videos; and stop-motion films with their Barbie and Ken dolls, said Erica Cassini, another friend.
Claire Murphy, Ms. Murphy’s mother and a school guidance counselor in Marietta, described her daughter as passionate.
“She does nothing halfway,” she said. “If she’s doing something, she’s going to do it all-out.”
Watching live baseball is so important to Mr. Sumers that the couple’s third date in 2011, to a Los Angeles Angels game, was a kind of test, which Ms. Murphy passed. It turns out she, too, is an avid fan of baseball and visiting ballparks.
“At the age of 5, he could watch a doubleheader, and then keep going,” said his father, Gary Sumers, who retired as a chief operating officer for the Blackstone Group, the New York investment firm. Each spring, the Sumers family, originally from Chicago and now New York City, traveled to Mesa, Ariz., to watch the Cubs.
Travel is an even bigger passion for Brian Sumers. For him, travel planning is not a pastime but a “disorder,” he said. Immediately after the couple’s ill-fated Wyoming journey, he booked Ms. Murphy and himself on a trip to Thailand and Hong Kong. In three years together, they have traveled America and across five continents.
Ms. Murphy, who has caught his travel bug and now writes a travel blog, said: “Travel doesn’t have to be this cattle-herd process. Now I don’t know any other way.”

Where she can sometimes be edgy and talkative, he is quieter and more outwardly reserved. “Brian is a man of few words,” said his mother, Benita Sakin, a lawyer.
Ms. Murphy’s mother said their contrasting personalities were nicely complementary. “I see how much they respect each other,” she said. “Brian helps Katie calm down, when she needs it. And I think Katie injects some energy into Brian, when he needs it. It’s a really good partnership.”
That partnership extends to supporting each other’s careers.
“I see the respect that Katie commands” in Hollywood, said Mr. Sumers, who has come to enjoy being her “plus one” companion at film premieres. “People want to talk to her. It’s impressive.”
Ms. Murphy added: “Brian is really proud of me. It’s nice to have someone who still finds it all exciting. Knowing Brian, I think that will never change.”
For their engagement in July 2013, as if to forever establish the centrality of the 2011 river accident in their life together, Mr. Sumers took Ms. Murphy back to that same Wyoming dude ranch.
Their wedding, on July 26, took place in a similarly remote and rustic setting: the Red Cliff Ranch in Heber Valley, Utah. Set on 175 acres in Lake Creek Canyon, the vista of the horse and cattle pasture leading to a lake was taken in by the couple’s 125 guests, as the Rocky Mountain peaks loomed in the distance. The bride’s brother, Michael G. Murphy, who became a Universal Life minister for the event, led the couple in their vows on a clearing in an aspen grove in front of steep red rock cliffs. Afterward, their friends and relatives (all of whom traveled from places outside Utah) dined in the ranch’s stable.
In his vows, Mr. Sumers promised to take his wife to “spring training every March and buy you unlimited hot dogs and beer.”
In hers, she choked up with emotion as she promised “to be the best mother possible to our children, and to raise our family in a home full of love and laughter.”
At their reception, they danced in the ranch’s open-air pavilion to “I Need You,” the Tim McGraw-Faith Hill duet, as Mr. Sumers unabashedly sang along:
“I wanna get lost in some corner booth, Cantina Mexico
I wanna dance to the static of an AM radio.”
Correction: August 22, 2014
An earlier version of this article misspelled the given name of one of Ms. Murphy’s friends.  She is Erica Cassini, not Erika.  The article also misspelled the city where the groom’s family would travel to watch the Cubs play.  It is Mesa, Ariz., not Meza.

Their plan seemed simple enough. One man stood outside Express Laundry in Brooklyn on Saturday. It was around midnight, and the coast was clear. He gave a partner a cue.
Then
a hooded accomplice
entered the laundromat, at 2149 Caton Avenue, southeast of Prospect Park, pulled out a gun and told an attendant to hand over the cash, said Jasmine Pierce, another laundry attendant, who had watched surveillance video of the robbery.
The robbers got away with about $500, she said.
The police said this was the latest of a dozen robberies or attempted robberies at laundromats and two at fast-food restaurants this year by a group of three men. No arrests have been made.
In each case, the robbers wore masks and gloves, and at least one of the men had a gun, according to a news release from the New York Police Department. The men then took money from the registers and from customers, the police said. There were no reports of injuries during the robberies. The police did not release the amounts of money taken.
Most of the businesses that were robbed were in Brooklyn, but two were in Queens, the police said. The robbery on Saturday night has taken a toll on Express Laundry, Ms. Pierce, 27, said. Business has dwindled, and she said some customers might be afraid to come back.
“There are significantly less people than usual,” Ms. Pierce said. “I don’t blame them, because who wants to go wash clothes where people pop in with guns at? They need to catch these guys.”
Still, she said: “People still need to wash and dry and fold their clothes. Or in other cases, they need someone — us — to do it for them.”
Isidro Diaz, the owner of Laundry Land Laundromat on Linden Boulevard in Brooklyn, said his night employee hid in a back room when a gunman barged into the lobby on March 18 and demanded cash.
“My night attendant called the police and hid with the detergents,” Mr. Diaz said. “At the end the guys didn’t take off with anything. But now we lock all the doors to our store. Usually, since we’re open 24 hours, that means our doors are physically open, too. Not anymore.”

THE proverbial $35 cup of coffee that can result from overspending a bank account hasn’t disappeared yet.
While some banks have made changes, half of the country’s big banks still may reorder your daily transactions in a way that maximizes the potential fees you will pay if you overdraw your account, according to a report released this week by the Pew Charitable Trusts.
The report scrutinized the practices of 44 of the largest banks based on deposits. It found that half still reorder transactions from the largest amount to the smallest amount — known as “high to low” posting — rather than processing them either from the smallest to largest amount, or in chronological order. High-to-low posting tends to result in fees being applied to several smaller amounts, rather than one fee applied to a single, larger amount.
Critics of the “high to low” practice say it can result in multiple charges set off by small purchases — as when buying a cup of coffee overdraws an account, resulting in a (typical) $35 overdraft fee.
“They can maximize overdraft fees doing it this way,” said Susan K. Weinstock, director of Pew’s safe checking project.
Banks have argued that high-to-low processing benefits customers because it makes sure that big, important payments — like rent, mortgages or student loans — are made first. But it also can result in multiple overdraft fees for unsuspecting customers. Consumers tend to keep track of spending chronologically, Ms. Weinstock said, so “having someone changing the order is really confusing and makes it hard to manage their account.”
The fees are especially a problem for younger and lower-income customers, who may not have the resources to keep a safe cash cushion in their accounts, she said.
As an example, the report compares two hypothetical examples using seven transactions — including an A.T.M. withdrawal, debit card purchases and a check — in a checking account with a $150 balance. If the transactions are processed in chronological order, the customer overdraws the account by roughly $39 and incurs a single $35 overdraft fee (the typical fee charged at large banks). If the same transactions are reordered from the highest amount to the lowest, however, the customer overdraws the account by the same $39, but is hit with four overdraft fees totaling $140.
William Terry, 35, a veterans counselor in North Wilmington, Del., said he once ended up paying $175 in overdraft fees, when a $500 car payment was deducted a day before a direct deposit arrived and overdrew his account. He could stomach one overdraft fee, he said, but his bank processed the car payment ahead of multiple smaller purchases that he had made earlier, and charged overdraft fees for all of them. “When I called,” he said, “they said I need to balance my budget better.”
After a spate of legal challenges over overdraft fees in recent years, some big banks began to drop the practice of high-to-low reordering.
Pew’s report recognized 22 banks for having the “best practice” of not reordering transactions from high to low. They include Citibank, which adopted a low-to-high processing sequence in 2011, as well as Ally Bank, U.S. Bank and Key Bank.
Continue reading the main story
Continue reading the main story
Some other big banks, like JPMorgan Chase, Bank of America and Wells Fargo, use “limited” reordering: They generally do not reorder debit card purchases and A.T.M. withdrawals, but may post certain debits, like checks, in high-to-low order, according to Pew. Thirty banks have set a negative balance threshold amount — typically $5 — for imposing an overdraft fee.
Four banks met all of Pew’s recommended “best” or “good” overdraft practices: Ally Bank, Charles Schwab Bank, OneWest Bank and USAA Federal Savings Bank.
Here are some additional questions about checking account practices:
■
How can I tell if my bank uses high-to-low ordering?
You can look at your account disclosure, often available on the bank’s website, for an explanation of how the bank processes deposits and withdrawals. More banks are adopting simplified disclosure boxes that make it easier to learn how accounts are managed.
Pew’s report
also includes a summary chart of the banks it studied.
■
Will the government set new rules for overdraft fees?
The Consumer Financial Protection Bureau is studying bank overdraft policies. Pew and other nonprofit groups, like the Center for Responsible Lending, are urging the bureau to adopt rules that require banks to process transactions in a way that does not maximize overdraft fees.
■
How can I minimize my risk of overdraft fees?
The Consumer Financial Protection Bureau advises setting up low-balance alerts, via text or email, which most banks offer. You can also opt out of overdraft coverage for debit card purchases and A.T.M. withdrawals; if a purchase would overdraw your account, it will be declined, rather than being approved with an overdraft fee. Some banks allow you to link your checking account to a savings account, and draw funds automatically if you overspend; there is often a fee for this, but it’s usually less than an overdraft fee.

Elizabeth Lauten, the congressional staff member who posted critical comments of Sasha and Malia Obama on her Facebook page late last week, has resigned.
Ms. Lauten, who was the communications director for Representative Stephen Fincher, Republican of Tennessee, posted the derogatory comments after the Obama daughters appeared with their father at the annual turkey pardoning on Wednesday. The daughters, as
our colleague Julie Hirschfeld Davis reported
, looked less than thrilled to be at the ceremony.
In the post, Ms. Lauten told the girls to “dress like you deserve respect, not a spot at a bar.”
“Dear Sasha and Malia, I get you’re both in those awful teen years, but you’re a part of the First Family, try showing a little class,” Ms. Lauten wrote.
The Facebook rant went viral and provoked a storm of criticism. Ms. Lauten quickly
posted an apology on Friday
, saying, “After many hours of prayer, talking to my parents, and re-reading my words online, I can see more clearly just how hurtful my words were.” She later made a majority of
her Facebook profile private
.
The resignation was first reported by
NBC News on Monday
.

Dr. Luis Etienne Tollinche and Peter Raymond Meyer were married Wednesday evening at Eleven Madison Park, a restaurant in New York. The Rev. Ed Ingebretsen, a priest affiliated with the American Catholic Church in the United States, officiated.
Dr. Tollinche, 39, is an anesthesiologist at Memorial Sloan-Kettering Cancer Center in New York. He graduated cum laude from Harvard and received a medical degree from Case Western Reserve University in Cleveland. He is a son of Maria P. Tollinche of Lewes, Del., and the late Dr. Charles R. Tollinche. His father retired as a general surgeon in Hoboken, N.J.
Mr. Meyer, 44, is a vice president and regional planning manager for Macy’s in New York, overseeing women’s shoes and jewelry for the company’s Northeast region. He graduated from Syracuse. He is the son of Donna F. Meyer and James M. Meyer of Cary, N.C. His mother retired as an accounting specialist with the Hoffman Auto Group, a group of car dealerships in Avon, Conn. His father retired as a sales executive with C. E. Bradley Laboratories, a paint manufacturer in Brattleboro, Vt.

IHT Archive
1939: Queen Mary’s Limousine Hit
LONDON — Queen Mary was badly bruised and shaken although she escaped serious injury this afternoon [May 23] when her old-fashioned limousine collided with a heavy truck and was hurled on its side at a road junction in southwest London. The Queen Mother, who will be seventy-two on Friday, climbed through a window of the wrecked sedan on a step- ladder brought by a house painter who happened to be working near by and rushed across the street.
White and dazed, Queen Mary was escorted to the house of a local physician a few yards from the scene of the accident. After a brief rest and a cup of tea with an aspirin, she was driven in another royal car back to her residence, Marlborough House, for an immediate examination by two of her personal physicians, Lord Dawson of Penn and Sir Stanley Hewett. Later, the following bulletin was issued over their signatures: ‘‘Her Majesty Queen Mary is suffering from bruising and shock as a result of an accident this afternoon in which her Majesty’s car was overturned.’’
1964: Ritual Dance Fire Kills 6
SAN FRANCISCO — Six persons died in a fire at a Roman Catholic parish hall last night [May 23] touched off by flames from a ritual fire dance of Samoans. About 100 persons were burned or injured in the rush from the building. The Samoans were marking a Polynesian holiday. A witness, Roy Brown, 35, said three dancers were performing on the stage of the hall when a fourth came on to light a knife, wrapped in cotton and soaked with gasoline, over a pan of gasoline. ‘‘I yelled to him ‘Don’t light it there!’ He’s kind of stubborn. He lit it and — whammo! He threw up his hands, dropped the knife and the pan exploded. We didn’t have a chance to stop it. The fire hit the curtains of the stage and that was it.’’ The sight of the spreading flames touched off a panicky rush for the front exit. The rear exit was blocked by a wall of flame from the stage. Firemen found the bodies strewn near the front. The dead were Samoans.

The Stone
is a forum for contemporary philosophers and other thinkers on issues both timely and timeless.
This is the seventh in a series of interviews about religion that I am conducting for The Stone. The interviewee for this installment is Tim Maudlin, a professor of philosophy at New York University and the author of “Philosophy of Physics: Space and Time.”
Gary Gutting
: Could you begin by noting aspects of recent scientific cosmology that are particularly relevant to theological questions?
No one looking at the completely random location of homo sapiens in the universe could seriously maintain that the whole thing was intentionally created for us.
Tim Maudlin
: That depends on the given theological account. The biblical account of the origin of the cosmos in Genesis, for example, posits that a god created the physical universe particularly with human beings in mind, and so unsurprisingly placed the Earth at the center of creation.
Modern cosmological knowledge has refuted such an account. We are living in the golden age of cosmology: More has been discovered about the large-scale structure and history of the visible cosmos in the last 20 years than in the whole of prior human history. We now have precise knowledge of the distribution of galaxies and know that ours is nowhere near the center of the universe, just as we know that our planetary system has no privileged place among the billions of such systems in our galaxy and that Earth is not even at the center of our planetary system. We also know that the Big Bang, the beginning of our universe, occurred about 13.7 billion years ago, whereas Earth didn’t even exist until about 10 billion years later.
No one looking at the vast extent of the universe and the completely random location of
homo sapiens
within it (in both space and time) could seriously maintain that the whole thing was intentionally created for us. This realization began with Galileo, and has only intensified ever since.
G.G.
: I don’t see why the extent of the universe and our nonprivileged spatio-temporal position within it says anything about whether we have some special role in the universe. The major monotheistic religions maintain that there is a special
spiritual
relationship between us and the creator. But that doesn’t imply that this is the only purpose of the universe or that we’re the only creatures with a special relationship to the creator.
T.M.
: Yes, of course, there are, in theory, other possible hypotheses about the origin of the universe and our role in it. Someone might hold that the universe was created with humans playing some important role, but a role equally played by other living beings (not living on Earth); or that the universe was created with some living beings playing an important role, but that humans are not among them; or even that the universe was created with no particular regard for any living beings.
If cosmology is to bear on any such hypothesis, then the hypothesis must lead to some expectations for the sort of universe a deity so motivated would create. The expectations following from the accounts, like Genesis, that make us the main purpose of the universe have, as I’ve pointed out, the great weight of evidence against them. (The other sorts of hypotheses have not been much advocated to my knowledge, and hence not developed to the point where one would know what sort of a physical universe to expect if any of them were true. My guess is that most religious people would not be especially interested in these hypotheses.)
G.G.
: I think we need to distinguish different sorts of theism. There are versions of theism that, like a literal reading of Genesis, are inconsistent with what we know about cosmology. But there are also versions that don’t require any specific story about the extent of the physical universe or our location in it. For example, there’s a basic theism that merely asserts that there’s an intelligent being that created the entire universe. It says nothing about what the purpose of God’s creation was, beyond simply making a universe. So I don’t see why every version of theism is refuted by scientific cosmology.
T.M.
: Theism, as religious people typically hold it, does not merely state that some entity created the universe, but that the universe was created specifically with humans in mind as the most important part of creation. If we have any understanding at all of how an intelligent agent capable of creating the material universe would act if it had such an intention, we would say it would not create the huge structure we see, most of it completely irrelevant for life on Earth, with the Earth in such a seemingly random location, and with humans appearing only after a long and rather random course of evolution.
G.G.
: Maybe, but that conclusion doesn’t follow from scientific cosmology; it’s based on further assumptions about what a creator would want — and how the creator would go about achieving it. Moreover, theistic religions can allow for many other intelligent creatures with a special relation to God; and it’s even plausible to think that God might have made a huge and complex universe as an object of knowledge for intelligent creatures. From that standpoint, the development of scientific cosmology would be part of God’s plan.
In any case, I’d like to hear your thoughts on a recent effort to find scientific support for religious views. Some theists have appealed to scientific cosmology to argue that there’s a “fine-tuning” of physical constants that shows that the universe is designed to support living beings and, in particular, humans. It’s said, for example, that if the ratio of the mass of the neutron to the mass of the proton were just slightly different, there couldn’t be sufficient structure to allow for the existence of organisms like us.
If there were some deity who desired that we know of its existence, there would be simple, clear ways to convey that information.
T.M.
: At this point, our physical theories contain quite a large number of “constants of nature,” of which we have no deeper account. There seem to be more of them than most physicists are comfortable with, and we don’t know for sure whether these “constants” are really constant rather than variable. This gives rise to questions about “fine-tuning” of these constants. One thing to keep in mind is that the true number and status of the “constants of nature” is not part of any well-established physical theory: It is part of what we don’t yet know rather than what we do know.
G.G.
: So are you saying that we don’t know enough about the relevant constants to get a theistic argument started?
T.M.
: Yes, since we don’t even know if the “constants” are constant, we certainly don’t know enough to draw any conclusions about the best account of why they have the particular values they have right now and around here. Since we don’t know how the various “constants” might be related to each other by deeper physics, the game of trying to figure out the effect of changing just one and leaving the rest alone also is not well founded.
One thing is for sure: If there were some deity who desired that we know of its existence, there would be simple, clear ways to convey that information. I would say that any theistic argument that starts with the constants of nature cannot end with a deity who is interested in us knowing of its existence.
G.G.
: Once again, that’s assuming we are good judges of how the deity would behave. But suppose that a surprisingly narrow range of the relevant constants turns out to be necessary for humans to exist. Some critics would say that even so, cosmological inflation would provide a satisfactory explanation with no reference to a creator. What’s your view on that?
T.M.
: Not everything about cosmology is known. We do not know how to reconcile quantum theory and relativity yet, and such a reconciliation would be needed to investigate the nature of the Big Bang. In particular, we don’t understand the basic physics well enough to tell if anything preceded the Big Bang. Even the existence of an inflationary period is still controversial.
One very speculative idea in cosmology is that the entire universe contains infinitely many “pocket universes” or “bubble universes,” in each of which the quantities we call “constants of nature” take different, randomly chosen, values. If so, then every possible combination of such values occurs somewhere, and living beings will obviously only evolve in regions where the combination of values supports life. Such an account predicts that intelligent creatures would arise in essentially random locations in a huge cosmological structure, just as we see. But this idea is highly speculative, and there is no direct evidence in its favor.
G.G.
: So is your view that we don’t currently know enough to decide whether or not fine-tuning for human life supports theism?
T.M.
: First, note how “humans” got put into that question! If there were any argument like this to be made, it would go through equally well for cockroaches. They, too, can only exist in certain physical conditions. The attempt to put
homo sapiens
at the center of this discussion is a reflection of our egocentrism, and has no basis at all in the actual structure of the universe.
Consider a different hypothesis. Suppose that there is a deity who created the universe with particular attention to the fate of some creatures in a distant galaxy. The very existence of the Earth and the evolution of life on Earth was just an unintended byproduct of setting up the “constants of nature” for the sake of those creatures, not us. That would be a fascinating thing to find out, but not what most people with interests in theism were after. The actual values of the “constants of nature” certainly cannot provide
more
evidence for their (Genesis-like) hypothesis than for this hypothesis.
G.G.
: Finally, let me ask about what I’ve called causal theism, which merely argues that a creator is needed to explain the very existence of the universe, regardless of its purpose. Some cosmologists, like Lawrence Krauss, have suggested that current physical theory shows how the universe could have emerged from nothing — for example, by a quantum fluctuation. What do you think of this suggestion?
Related
More From The Stone
Read previous contributions to this series.
T.M.
: The more general claim that a creator is needed to explain the very existence of the universe is a much, much weaker claim, and is consistent with humanity having had no particular significance at all to the creator. That’s why I say that just getting some creator or other is not what most people are after.
In any case, does there need to be a nonmaterial cause as an explanation for the entire material universe? Causal explanation either goes on forever backward in time or it comes to a stop somewhere. Even people who want to postulate a nonmaterial cause of the material universe often see no need to invoke yet another cause for that nonmaterial cause, and so are content to let the sequence of causal explanations come to an end. But the initial state of the universe (if there is one) could just as well be the uncaused cause. Or if there is no initial state, and the universe goes back infinitely in time, then it can’t have a cause that precedes it in time.
Krauss does not suggest that the universe came to exist “from nothing” in the sense of “did not come from anything at all,” but rather that it came from a quantum vacuum state. He seems to think that such a vacuum state would be a satisfying place to end the causal regress as the state with no causal antecedent. The vacuum state has many important symmetries, so if one could tell a physical story of everything coming out of a vacuum state it would have a certain appealing plausibility. But one could still ask, “Why start with the vacuum state rather than something else?” I think we don’t know enough to make any plausible guess about even whether there was an initial state, much less what it might have been. This goes beyond what we have good evidence or theory for.
G.G.
: You obviously don’t see scientific cosmology as supporting any case for theism. You also think that it refutes theistic religions’ claiming that the primary purpose of God’s creation is the existence of human beings. What, finally, is your view about the minimal theistic view that the universe was created by an intelligent being (regardless of its purpose). Does scientific cosmology support the atheistic position that there is no such creator or does it leave us with the agnostic judgment that there isn’t sufficient evidence to say?
T.M.
: Atheism is the default position in any scientific inquiry, just as a-quarkism or a-neutrinoism was. That is, any entity has to earn its admission into a scientific account either via direct evidence for its existence or because it plays some fundamental explanatory role. Before the theoretical need for neutrinos was appreciated (to preserve the conservation of energy) and then later experimental detection was made, they were not part of the accepted physical account of the world. To say physicists in 1900 were “agnostic” about neutrinos sounds wrong: they just did not believe there were such things.
As yet, there is no direct experimental evidence of a deity, and in order for the postulation of a deity to play an explanatory role there would have to be a lot of detail about how it would act. If, as you have suggested, we are not “good judges of how the deity would behave,” then such an unknown and unpredictable deity cannot provide good explanatory grounds for any phenomenon. The problem with the “minimal view” is that in trying to be as vague as possible about the nature and motivation of the deity, the hypothesis loses any explanatory force, and so cannot be admitted on scientific grounds. Of course, as the example of quarks and neutrinos shows, scientific accounts change in response to new data and new theory. The default position can be overcome.
This interview was conducted by email and edited. Previous interviews in this series were with
Alvin Plantinga
,
Louise Antony
,
John D. Caputo
,
Howard Wettstein
,
Jay L. Garfield
and
Philip Kitcher
.
Gary Gutting is a professor of philosophy at the University of Notre Dame, and an editor of
Notre Dame Philosophical Reviews
. He is the author of, most recently, “Thinking the Impossible: French Philosophy Since 1960″ and writes regularly for The Stone.

The small island of Isla Mujeres, off the coast of Cancún, Mexico, is getting big dose of art with the recent inauguration of “Sea Walls: Murals for Oceans,” a display of 15 large-scale murals by some of the top names in contemporary street art.
The project was organized by
PangeaSeed,
a Hawaii-based nonprofit that raises awareness for the conservation of sharks and other marine species around the world, and the goal is to educate both local residents and tourists about conservation efforts in the area.
The group’s founder, Tre’ Packard, said that the close to five-mile-long island was chosen for the works because whale sharks, which are overfished and an endangered species, migrate off its coast from May to September.
“It’s one the largest gatherings of these animals in the world, and these artworks are a form of activism,” he said in a phone interview.
The images, which will hang permanently in the island’s downtown area, are all different renditions of the sharks and of manta rays, also an endangered species.
Tristan Eaton, a Los Angeles-based artist whose work is part of the permanent collection of the Museum of Modern Art in New York, for example, created a 130-foot multicolored collage with different paintings that comprise a shark silhouette.
To prepare for the project, the artists visited the island and had two days to swim and interact with both varieties of animals.
Other participants include Yoh Nagao from Japan, Pelucas from Spain and Cinzah Seekayem from New Zealand.

A successful libertarian politician is a creature of nuance. After all, he is drawing a government salary to get rid of government, without confusing his audience.
Speaking to a crowd of largely young, conservative techies in San Francisco on Saturday, the Republican and self-described libertarian Senator Rand Paul showed a lot of finesse in balancing opposites. It might also have been a preview of the kind of balancing he’ll repeat nationally, as he ponders a 2016 presidential run.
Libertarians would win popular support, he said, “if we convince people we’re not opposed to government, we just need it minimized.”
Dressed in faded jeans and a crisp white button-down shirt, Mr. Paul said Internet technology makes obsolete many government functions almost by existing.
“The crowd wants good service — you rate your Uber driver, your stay at a hotel,” he said. “As information becomes more widespread, maybe you need less and less government.”
There were fewer specifics than ideas during his appearance, which consisted of a 30-minute talk followed by a panel. The event was “
2014: Reboot
,” a conference organized by
Lincoln Labs
, a group that tries to draw techies to conservative politics.
The government, he said, should find and foster superstar teachers to wow students with online appearances. Yet in the classrooms “there will still be teachers, reinforcing the lectures and handing out homework,” he said.
It was not clear how that would lead to less government, or how the government would find what he described as “the LeBron James” of pedagogy.
Mr. Paul reiterated his admiration for
Bitcoin
, the digital cryptocurrency, but indicated it could be better utilized as a proxy for corporations. “I’m not sure how or if it succeeds ultimately, but it’s cool to think that Walmart, with a 3 or 4 percent margin, could double it” by foregoing what it pays credit card companies, instead offering a kind of digital currency backed by corporate equity.
A previous version of this idea rested on a basket of equities, but now Mr. Paul would let a hundred corporate coins be coded. “Wal-coin, Kmart-coin, turn it in for Walmart stock, Kmart stock,” Mr. Paul said. “I think it’s coming.” He did not say how, by attaching Bitcoin to regulated securities, the stateless digital currency would not be inside the purview of the Securities and Exchange Commission.
Such quibbles mattered little to the audience, which was particularly receptive to Mr. Paul’s criticism of the National Security Agency. He limited the criticism to how the N.S.A. goes about spying on people, not the spying itself. “I’m all for sifting through information” to catch terrorists, he said.
Above all, the libertarian and the techies seemed to converge in one area: Silicon Valley’s irrepressible self-belief augmented by the prospect of growing libertarian power.
“That’s what I love about coming out here,” Mr. Paul said. “You create something out of nothing.”

For the last 24 hours, I’ve been watching my Twitter feed tick by with mentions of Ello, a new social network that is some amalgam of Twitter, Facebook and Medium, the online publishing start-up, rolled into one.
The main attraction, according to the company, is that unlike Facebook or Twitter, Ello will remain ad-free, ad infinitum. And right now, the
debate  in Silicon Valley
circles —
Wired
,
The Wall Street Journal
,
Re/code
—  is whether that claim is unrealistic.
Here’s another question: Why all the fuss?
Here’s one guess: Never underestimate the power of the velvet rope.
Let’s tease that out a bit. Start-ups introduce new products nearly every day, and many are indistinguishable from the last. Ello, while a new kid on the block, is hardly different than dozens of failed past social ventures;
Ello’s manifesto
feels like a carbon copy of the
mission statement from App.net
, a failed social start-up.
But like some other start-ups have in the past, Ello used a clever trick to grow its user base: An invite-only new user system. And there’s nothing like the fear of missing out — sometimes called FOMO — to get a crowd worked up about your new product. That goes double for Silicon Valley types, who live and die by how early they can adopt the latest trend.
Techies took to Twitter — an advertising-supported social network, mind you — to beg for Ello invitations. (I admit it, I broke down and asked for one too.)
It is all part of a hype cycle that spreads early word-of-mouth advertising for companies that would probably receive little attention otherwise.
Rekindle
, a Tinder clone, forces you to stay on a two-week waiting list unless you tweet about the app. Mailbox, an email app which ended up getting bought by online storage start-up Dropbox, generated hype by forcing users to wait in a line thousands of users long.
One reason for the strategy is technical. If you’re a small company like Ello, it’s difficult to bring in a host of new people and keep the site from crashing. A metered approach is smart. But a potential upside is fostering anticipation among venture capitalists, trendsetters and journalists.
Now is when the real challenge comes, though, making people want to continue using the new network once they’ve been let inside. Otherwise, it’ll look like so many other failed social networks: Something like a nightclub with a long line outside but no one on the dance floor inside.

Travelers who can’t sit still have a new outings option. Two high-profile brands in the active lifestyle and fitness worlds are teaming up to offer
Reebok Fitness Vacations
by Austin Adventures.
The inaugural vacation, a six-day trip in Montana this summer, includes hiking in the backcountry of Yellowstone National Park, trail running past hot springs, and kayaking Class II and III rapids under the shadow of the Absaroka Range.
Three itineraries are scheduled in 2014, with a full launch scheduled for 2015.
This season’s trips include two Montana outings, two tours at Bryce and Zion national parks in Utah, and a November trip to Costa Rica.
In general, the vacations will include a wide range of activities such as biking, hiking, rafting, yoga, traditional and forest trail running, functional fitness classes, group fitness classes and obstacle racing.
Guests on some itineraries may engage a masseuse, yoga instructor or other fitness expert traveling with the group. Menus are intended to be nutrition-oriented.
Prices range from $2,498 to $3,898, based on double occupancy.

He’s tackled aliens, gangsters, androids, gladiators, the military and Robin Hood. Now the director Ridley Scott takes on the Bible with “
Exodus: Gods and Kings
.” The film, opening Dec. 12, turns the story of Moses (Christian Bale) and his uprising against the Egyptian pharaoh (Joel Edgerton) into an adventure tale. The film, like the original story, may be an epic, but Mr. Scott’s approach was decidedly more practical.
“I tend to be someone who comes from the reality of things,” Mr. Scott said in a phone interview. “I try to reach into the information that is there and latch onto what could have been real.”
That was his thinking when it came to depicting the 10 plagues that befall the Egyptians. Though he was trying to stay true to a story about faith, Mr. Scott wanted the plagues unfold in a natural way.
Here is a closer look at four of the film’s plagues, with Mr. Scott, and the visual effects supervisor Peter Chiang, explaining what it took to bring a series of disasters to the big screen.
The Nile Bleeds
In Mr. Scott’s take, the plagues are a progression of calamities, stemming from an inciting event: crocodiles attacking fishermen on boats and turning on one another. With so much carnage, a bloody river must follow.
“I got that from National Geographic,” Mr. Scott said, “where I’d seen a frenzy of crocs going after some poor buffalo.”
Much of that carnage is more implied than seen. To maintain the film’s PG-13 rating, human bloodletting isn’t depicted. The visual effects team created fluid simulations of blood leaking from completely computer-generated crocodiles.
“Most of the red featured in the Nile sequence is digital,” Mr. Chiang said in a phone interview. Coloring the water directly would have been a much greater logistical and visual challenge.
“It’s amazing,” he added, “how much reflections and light hide the color of water.”
Bring On The Frogs
Because the bloody river kills many of the creatures in the river, frogs come to the surface hunting for food. Here, a combination of digital and practical means were used.
“We started off by Ridley choosing the species of frog he wanted to be represented,” Mr. Chiang said. “It was a darker species.”
Some 400 live frogs, monitored by six frog handlers, were brought on set. In this sequence, the creatures were placed on the actress
Golshifteh Farahani
and emptied on her head.
But more were needed. “When you put 400 live frogs on a set, they kind of fit on a table and don’t look that amazing,” Mr. Chiang said.
So the frogs became references for the digital re-creations they would make. They tweaked the animation to make the frogs more energetic, as the real amphibians were a bit stubborn and slow. In the resulting sequence, it is difficult to tell the live hoppers from the digital ones.
Get Ready to Swat
When the frogs die, the flies come. And locusts follow. Mr. Chiang and his team looked at the nature movies of David Attenborough. “In those films, a lot of flies literally fly up to the lens,” he said.
Their goal was to immerse the viewer, so they created a close-proximity layer of flies out of focus so that the audience would feel surrounded by the cloud.
The filmmakers also felt it necessary to see streams of flies in the background, so they cheated on the scales and perspectives, Mr. Chiang said. “In certain scenes, because of the wide-angle lenses, we created flies the size of cats.”
With the locusts, the filmmakers went with a different kind of cheating, animating them to fly much faster than normal to give the insects a more menacing feel.

In the latest in a series of measures tightening state control over the media, the Chinese government is requiring journalists to sign a secrecy agreement with their employers as a condition for obtaining press credentials.
China’s top media regulator, the State Administration of Press, Publication, Radio, Film and Television,
said
on Monday that the agreements would obligate the journalists “not to disclose any information acquired in the course of work, without permission from employers.” It also announced that for the first time it would issue credentials to journalists working for news websites.
The process of renewing credentials took effect on Tuesday and will end on Oct. 30. By then about 250,000 journalists are expected to obtain new credentials,
according to Guangming Daily
, a state-run newspaper.
The media agency oversees all Chinese journalists working in mainland China.
Every five years
since 1989, the administration has issued new press credentials, which Chinese journalists must have in order to attend many news conferences and to conduct interviews with government officials.
The agency’s supervision over journalists has increased significantly since Xi Jinping became China’s president in March 2013. In January, in preparation for issuing the new press credentials, the agency required all Chinese journalists to take an
exam
, which tasked the journalists with memorizing Chinese leaders’ comments about regulating the news media.
In April 2013, the media administration
banned
journalists from publishing online any information acquired while on the job unless they had clearance from their employers. And late last month it published a
directive
banning journalists from sharing information with foreign news agencies.
Chinese journalists, operating under strict censorship, sometimes pass information they cannot run in their own publications to foreign journalists or post the information online.
In May, Gao Yu, 70, a veteran Chinese journalist and longtime government critic, was
detained
on charges of leaking secret documents to a foreign website.

WASHINGTON — Complex financial instruments that helped James H. Simons’ Renaissance Technologies claim more than $6 billion in tax savings were “extremely important,” its co-chief executive of the hedge fund said at a Senate hearing on Tuesday.
Peter Brown, co-chief executive  of Renaissance, said the financial products gave firm the ability to get stronger returns by borrowing more.
“On an unlevered basis, our models produce modest returns with very low volatility,” Mr. Brown testified at a hearing of the Senate Permanent Subcommittee on Investigations.
The subcommittee is holding a hearing after it found that more than a dozen hedge funds used structures called “barrier” or “basket” options to claim tax savings and borrow more money
Representatives from Barclays and Deutsche Bank have also been called to the hearing. The subcommittee, led by Senator Carl Levin, Democrat of Michigan, has been investigating tax-avoidance strategies of several large United States companies.
“In essence, today’s hearing is about a series of fictions, one piled on top of another, fictions that major banks and their hedge fund clients used to avoid taxes and federal leverage limits,”Mr. Levin, who is chairman of the Senate subcommittee, said on Tuesday.
Earlier in the hearing, Steven M. Rosenthal, a senior fellow at the Urban-Brookings Tax Policy Center, called the options “tax alchemy” that helped the hedge funds characterize incorrectly what they were doing – in effect making millions of trades over a short period of time.
Mr. Brown countered assertions by Mr. Levin and Mr. Rosenthal that that these complicated structures  were used with the primary objective of skirting higher tax rates.
Mr. Brown said the options gave Renaissance Technologies the ability to protect against model and programming failures.
He described a “wake-up” call when Renaissance models failed in March 2000 as the dot-com bubble was bursting and the firm took heavy losses.
“It just never occurred to us or to our models that these markets could diverge so rapidly.” Mr. Brown said, adding that six months after that experience, Renaissance entered its first barrier option.
The hearing comes as Washington is fiercely debating tax policy reform. In a twist, Mr. Brown made his own comment on tax reform.
“I will only point out that if there were no rate differential between long-term and short-term capital gains, the tax code would be far simpler and far more certain.”

Brewster Kahle is a librarian by training and temperament. In the mid-1990s, when many saw the nascent World Wide Web as a place to sell things, he saw it as data that cried out to be preserved and cataloged. Later, he widened his scope to include material — film, books, music — that was not native to the web but could be digitally gathered there.
By most standards, Mr. Kahle has been pretty successful.
The Internet Archive
serves from two to three million visitors a day with such tools as the Wayback Machine, which provides snapshots of 435 billion Web pages saved over time. The archive has seven million texts (you could call them books), 2.1 million audio recordings, and 1.8 million videos. It is an immense library.
Mr. Kahle has even bigger dreams, however. With a limited staff, the archive can conserve only so much. But if anyone can become a curator, the archive may one day resemble one of those Borgesian fantasies of the Total Library, a place that not only collects the world but becomes it.
“We thought the machines were going to save us — crawling the web, digitizing the books, organizing the information — but we were wrong,” Mr. Kahle said. “Communities of people are at the heart of curation.”
At an event Tuesday night at the converted San Francisco church that serves as the archive’s headquarters, the nonprofit’s staff showed off exactly how it and communities are going to be “building libraries together,” as the catchphrase of the evening had it. An ample crowd was in attendance, despite San Francisco battling it out in the World Series at that very moment.
A new book scanner was presented; Robert Miller, the archive’s director of books, literally unveiled it. This baby was only 40 inches tall and 62 pounds, versus the earlier version’s six feet and 350 pounds. In other words, it is portable, and can be taken to collections that are too fragile or cumbersome to make their own way to the archive. It’s much easier to use, too.
Television is getting increasing emphasis at the archive, with hundreds of thousands of news reports that are searchable. Now, it is focusing on political ads as well.
Roger Macdonald, director of the Television Archive, introduced the
Philly Political Media Watch Project
, a pilot program by the archive, the Sunlight Foundation, Philadelphia’s Committee of Seventy (a government watchdog), the Linguistic Data Consortium at the University of Pennsylvania, and the University of Delaware’s Center for Community Research and Service.
The notion here is that political ads fly by on television, giving the viewer little context in which to judge them. Like the ad shown to the archive crowd where a Philadelphia candidate accused his opponent of burning down a church. Trumped-up charge or heinous act? No one had a clue.
The project is experimenting with creating a resource library of political ads and the TV news broadcasts in which they appeared. The goal is to help journalists and researchers compare the ads from a single sponsor or group, see who is paying for them and, ultimately, try to determine how well the local news media are covering the issues behind the ads. If the pilot works out in Philadelphia, it will unroll nationally in time for the 2016 elections.
“This is a modest step to providing individual citizens with the information they need,” Mr. Macdonald said.
A third area of focus is music
, which is expanding on previous efforts. In a throwback of sorts to an earlier era, a prototype listening room has been set up at the archive for physical patrons.
“Preserving music is now urgent as tapes are disintegrating, disc formats are used less, and new web-only music has blossomed,” Mr. Kahle said. “We are now working with top archives, collectors and labels to preserve our treasures.” Among them: the
ARChive of Contemporary Music
and
Musica Omnia
, a label specializing in classical music.
Yet another new archive effort involves the website. The existing site “kind of looks like it’s a 10-year-old website, because it’s a 10-year-old website,” Mr. Kahle explained. A
new site is in beta
. It prominently encourages users to use the site to assemble their own virtual libraries of material, which they can return to when they want to hear, for instance, Cuban jazz from the 1940s or read “Constitutional Thought in Sixteenth-Century France: A Study in the Evolution of Ideas.”
“It turns out people want old stuff, which I think is a good thing,” Mr. Kahle said.
Related Coverage:
In a Flood Tide of Digital Data, an Ark Full of Books
By DAVID STREITFELD
As society embraces all forms of digital entertainment, a latter-day Noah is looking the other way. Brewster Kahle, who runs the Internet Archive, a nonprofit, hopes to collect one copy of every book.

The Standard & Poor’s 500-stock index rose above 2,000 for the first time ever on Monday after a meeting of central bankers opened the door to more stimulus measures.
The new high for the benchmark S.&P. 500 index is only the latest sign of the buoyancy in the financial markets despite questions about the underlying strength of the economy.
At a meeting sponsored by the Federal Reserve in Jackson Hole, Wyo., on Friday, the president of the European Central Bank, Mario Draghi, suggested in a speech after the European markets had closed that the Continent’s policy makers needed to shift their focus away from budget cutting and focus on growth in the face of continued economic weakness.
European stocks jumped after the opening on Monday, with the Euro Stoxx 50 index up more than 2 percent at mid-day. In the United States, stocks rose, though less sharply, immediately after the opening bell.
The S.&P. 500 stood at 2,001.60 at mid-day, up 0.7 percent, or 13 points from Friday’s close. The Dow Jones industrial average rose 0.7 percent, or 113 points, to 17,113.80, while the Nasdaq composite index climbed 0.6 percent, or 29 points, to 4,567.50.
The rise in equity prices came despite some disappointing economic data showing that growth in the housing market was cooling.
Central bankers from around the world gathered over the weekend in Jackson Hole. The chairwoman of the Federal Reserve, Janet Yellen, gave little indication in her speech that the Fed is planning to further reduce its bond buying programs in the near future.
Stocks were also helped up by news of the latest corporate deals. Shares of Burger King Worldwide rose sharply after the company confirmed that it is in talks to buy the Canadian doughnut-and-coffee, Tim Hortons.

There is no shortage of spherical pendant lights, but there are few with gaping holes in their structure. Neil Poulton, a Scottish-born designer in Paris, has produced an example with tiny LEDs tucked within the frame. The fixture, named Scopas after the Greek sculptor who lived in the fourth century B.C. and who carved a much-copied personification of desire, is composed of black and white painted aluminum circles and is about 20 inches in diameter.
Visually startling — Artemide, the Italian company that manufacturers it, is not afraid to drop references to “Star Wars” — the $5,090 lamp looks strikingly different depending on the angle from which it is viewed. Scopas can be ordered at Artemide’s New York showroom at 46 Greene Street (Grand Street). Information: 212-925-1588.

By
JOYCE WADLER

Ever since the release of the “
Fifty Shades of Grey” trailer
, which opens with the gorgeous heroine interviewing a cool, controlling titan of industry who will ultimately initiate her into S&M, people have been asking me, “Is journalism really like that?” and I say, “Absolutely.”
True, the reporter they are asking about, Anastasia Steele, played by Dakota Johnson, is merely a novice, doing an article for a college newspaper. But the sequence of events — interviewing a spectacularly rich guy; being relentlessly pursued by him — is spot on.
In my 45 years in the business, it’s happened to me and other reporters countless times. This is why you see so many limos idling in front of The New York Times building, waiting to take us all to the Hamptons.
Also, unlike many films involving journalism, this one shows a reporter actually taking notes. And though visibly nervous, this young woman is tough. During her interview with Mr. Grey, Ms. Steele boldly asks, “Do you have any interests outside of work?” This is not a reporter you want to tangle with, except between the sheets, which, as I say, happens constantly in our business.
Mr. Grey, played by Jamie Dornan, provides an eerily accurate picture of a modern-day mogul as well. Consider the power and cadence of his remarks.
“ I don’t do” — pause — “romance.”
“My tastes are very” — pause — “singular.”
Wouldn’t we all like to have that confidence! It is Restaurant Week in New York, when chefs like to give full rein to their creativity whether the customer is up to it or not, but thanks to Mr. Grey’s example, I could finally hold my ground.
“I don’t do” — pause — “grilled watermelon.”
“My tastes are very” — pause — “singular. Could you just get me some meat and potatoes?”
But let’s be honest: Nobody really cares about how realistic a portrayal of journalism or the business world “Fifty Shades of Grey” is going to be. When people ask me about the trailer, I understand they are really saying, “Joyce, you are a
woman of the world
, and the popularity of ‘Fifty Shades of Grey,’ makes it abundantly clear that I have to get on the S&M bandwagon, though what their theme song would be I do not know. Can you give me some advice so I can tell what my partner really wants?”
Continue reading the main story
And so, readers, your darkest questions, anticipated and answered:
I have never done S&M, or B&D, but I did have to call AAA Sunday morning when my battery died. The mechanic and I experienced a powerful attraction to each other and went to a wooded park, where we made love so vigorously the woodland creatures gathered around and placed bets. (Where Thumper picked up such language, I do not know.)
To get into the “Fifty Shades of Grey” spirit as the day wore on, I told my new friend to close his eyes, then attached one end of the jumper cables to his toe and the other to the battery of his truck. Then I kicked over the engine. He hasn’t moved since. What can I do to revive him?

Messed Up in Middletown
Dear Messed Up:
I cannot say it too often: Read the manual. This was an advanced move and I daresay you did not do your homework. Otherwise, you would know never to jump-start a man unless he is grounded. This relationship cannot be revived. And neither can the fellow. Dig a shallow grave, pay off the rabbits, and don’t beat yourself up over this. Unless you think you’d like it.
I understand there was some controversy about the trailer, and some scenes were cut for TV. Do you know what they were?
Curious in Quebec
Dear Curious:
It was Christian Grey’s erotically charged scene about his sexual tastes. Originally Mr. Grey said, “My tastes are very” — pause — “singular. I only use Italian eye cream for those little lines that are already appearing around my eyes. I fret about them so.” But the idea of a man using any cream on his face was repugnant to audiences in test screenings, so the line was cut. I hear they’re keeping it for the European version.
The other night in bed, my partner told me that maybe I should pick up some bungee cords to secure the bike rack to the car. Then she winked salaciously and held her hands stiffly at her side and said, “And we could use it for other stuff, too.” Is she hinting she would like to explore bondage?
Flummoxed in Philadelphia
Dear Flummoxed:
No, she is hinting that she would like a new bike. The hands held tightly at one’s side signifies being secured to a gurney after breaking your shoulder because a tourist backed into you while taking a selfie. It is important to understand the cues.
I haven’t read the book, but there is a scene in the trailer of “Fifty Shades of Grey” which I hope you can explain to me. The young woman, Ms. Steele, is about to get on a helicopter. Mr. Grey is waiting for her with an expectant look on his face. I suspect something very hot is about to happen, but I cannot imagine what it is. Can you enlighten me?
Hopeful in Hackensack
Dear Hopeful:
You are a perceptive viewer. Something powerfully erotic is indeed about to occur. I cannot go into the most salacious details here, but as is suggested by the Grey Properties logo, the aircraft is a privately owned helicopter. Although she is standing still in the trailer, in a few moments Ms. Steele will carefully lift one leg and move it forward, placing it down inside the aircraft. Then she will execute the same shameless move with the other leg.
Let me be more explicit: Ms. Steele is about to step into a helicopter owned by the man she is dating. (Forgive me, I am getting a little excited.)
I have never been lucky enough to have this experience, but I gather that once you step inside these babies, they go anywhere you like. You will never be stuck in traffic on the Long Island Expressway or anywhere else every again.
(PANT! HEAVE! PANT! Where is that inhaler I got when the doctors thought I had asthma? I am so aroused. Why don’t I ever meet men with their own aircraft?)
Anyway, Hopeful, they say riding in a privately owned helicopter is so thrilling, so overwhelming, that once you do it you can never again fly commercial.
(YES, A PRIVATE PLANE, YES, YES, YES!!!!)
Excuse me. I need to go lie down.

It’s almost over: That ever-present, ever-looping Christmas song season.
We listen to the interminable cascade on store sound systems, the car radio and music-streaming services, but how much of it do we really like?
It’s a matter of personal taste, of course. Some like the religious canon, others the classics written by Jewish songwriters in snowless Los Angeles in the 1940s and 1950s. These days it may sound like Michael Bublé and Mariah Carey are slugging it out to replace Bing Crosby and Andy Williams at the top of the Christmas song list.
But when people actually get to register a preference, as they do on a streaming service like
Pandora
, a different, and surprising, set of songs and performers emerges. Pandora parsed its data to see what were the most popular songs in each state.
Pandora’s listeners are able to tap a thumbs-up or a thumbs-down icon for each song they stream. Pandora tracks that. It says,
nationwide, among the most “thumbed-up” songs
is Mariah Carey’s “
All I Want for Christmas Is You,
” though the melancholy “
Have Yourself a Merry Little Christmas
,”
first sung by Judy Garland
, is No. 1. The state popularity list shows the most popular songs that deviate significantly from the national taste. So we get a list with more regional variation.
There are some caveats that make this slightly less than an all-time authoritative list. Pandora’s listeners, though they number 76 million worldwide, skew young. And in states where Pandora has fewer listeners, the results may be less representative.
Classics remain popular. The Temptations’ version of
“Silent Night”
tops the list in seven states, most of them Southern: Georgia, Maryland, Mississippi, North Carolina, South Carolina, Tennessee and Virginia as well as the District of Columbia.
The country singer Faith Hill’s
“Where Are You Christmas”
is among the top three songs in 14 states and tops the list in four: Iowa, South Dakota, West Virginia and Kentucky.
Brenda Lee’s “
Rockin’ Around the Christmas Tree
,” from 1958, is the top choice in New Mexico, Oklahoma, Oregon and Washington.
But Pandora says the song that is No. 1 in the most states is
“Christmas Canon”
by Trans-Siberian Orchestra. It’s most popular across a broad swath of Middle America: Arkansas, Kansas, Michigan, Minnesota, Missouri, North Dakota, Ohio, Pennsylvania and Wisconsin. The group’s “
Christmas Eve/Sarajevo 12/24
” tops Wyoming’s and Colorado’s lists and is among the top three in six other states.
What about Bing Crosby and “Silent Night”? Barely registers. (The Drifters’
version
is No. 1 in Illinois, however.) Or what about “Christmas (Baby Please Come Home),” which
Rolling Stone
once said was the greatest Christmas song? It is widely popular, but it is the
Mariah Carey version
, not the 1963 version by Darlene Love, that resonates, especially in Massachusetts and New Jersey, where it is No. 1. (It turns out that Massachusetts also ranked Ms. Love’s version as its No. 3 choice.)
Another Christmas classic update is broadly popular. Leon Redbone and Zooey Deschanel’s version of “
Baby It’s Cold Outside
” aces out versions by Dean Martin, Michael Bublé, the gang from “Glee” and others. It’s No. 1 in Montana and is in the top three in five other states.
On the other hand, it’s not Madonna’s “Santa Baby” that is favored by Pandora listeners but the racier 1953
Eartha Kitt version
. Californians and Texans ranked it as their No. 1 Christmas song.
The top song in New York is “
Happy Xmas (War Is Over)
” by John Lennon.

“The Internet is becoming the center of the new work environment, not the corporate network,” he said. “The model of logging onto the corporate network is going away.”
That has yielded significant growth over Ping’s lifespan, Mr. Durand said. Its sales have grown on average about 50 percent a year, though he declined to provide more precise figures.
It’s a product offering that drew the attention of K.K.R., the investment giant best known for big leveraged buyouts like the takeovers of RJR Nabisco and the hospital operator
HCA
. But the firm has been dipping its toe more into the increasingly popular “growth equity” market, a somewhat nebulous term that encompasses investments in older, more established start-ups.
Several months ago, K.K.R. learned that Ping was looking to raise money with the assistance of the
Royal Bank of Canada
, and the firm’s technology co-head, Herald Chen, began pursuing meetings with Mr. Durand. To help its cause, the investment firm enlisted Mr. Doll, a former chief executive of the software encryption company PGP.
Over three months or so, the two sides began putting together what became the fund-raising round, with Mr. Chen and Mr. Doll agreeing to join Ping’s board.
“It’s pretty simple,” Mr. Chen said. “We have looked at a lot of investments in the growth area, and we think the Ping opportunity offers a great risk-reward proposition for K.K.R. We think it’s a solid investment.”
What K.K.R. will help bring to the table, according to both Mr. Chen and Mr. Doll, is its huge size and experience and contacts within the Fortune 100. (The firm’s stable of portfolio companies will also use Ping products.)
Though Ping remains focused on growth, Mr. Durand added that the company is also keeping an eye on metrics befitting a more mature company, like profits and cash flow. Those measures will become increasingly important as the company heads toward what he said was “independence” — which includes a potential initial public offering within a few years’ time.
Mr. Durand said that a prospective I.P.O. has been discussed internally over the last year and a half, though such a move is likely to require “a substantial company” if it is to succeed in the public markets. The job now, he added, is to keep getting Ping to that point, especially while security remains a central focus for both customers and investors.
“Now is the time to seize the moment,” he said.

WASHINGTON — In response to intelligence reports about plots being hatched by Al Qaeda’s affiliate in Yemen, the United States has, for the first time, asked officials at more than a dozen foreign airports to confiscate from passengers any electronic devices that cannot be turned on, American officials said on Monday.
The reports indicate that the terrorist group,
Al Qaeda in the Arabian Peninsula
, was trying to disguise explosives in devices like cellphones after repeated failed attempts to bring down an American airliner.
Its chief bomb maker has created several undetectable explosives in recent years that have led the United States to alter screening procedures.
“Aviation remains an attractive target to global terrorists, who are consistently looking for ways to circumvent our aviation security measures,” said one senior American official who did not want to be quoted by name discussing national security measures.
The United States does not handle the passenger screening on American-bound flights from overseas. But foreign airports have to meet a series of requirements from the
Department of Homeland Security
and the
Transportation Security Administration
in checking such passengers before they board.
The new measures apply to some airports in Europe, the Middle East and Africa. Passengers will have to turn on the electronic devices while being screened by security personnel to prove that the devices are harmless, the T.S.A. said Sunday. The fear is that unresponsive phones have been hollowed out and filled with explosives. It is considered next to impossible to detonate such devices at the checkpoints because they require external triggering mechanisms.
If an initial attempt fails to power up a device, passengers will be allowed to use a charger. If that fails, the devices will be confiscated. The T.S.A. said similar screening might be extended to other electronic devices like laptops and tablets. Some countries may also add security measures of their own.
It is not clear what will happen to the confiscated devices.
The T.S.A. did not say how long the new policy would remain in place, nor would it specify the countries or airports that would be affected. Last week,
the Department of Homeland Security said
that it had called for tightened passenger screening on many United States-bound flights from 15 airports in Europe, Africa and the Middle East, although it did not say which airports.
Since 2009, the Qaeda affiliate in Yemen has unsuccessfully planned at least three attacks on airliners using bombs carried on board or planted in cargo.
The bomb maker,
Ibrahim Hassan al-Asiri
, has trained a group of protégés who are prepared to take his place should he be captured or killed, American officials said. The Yemen group also has ties to some rebel groups in Syria that are fighting the government of President Bashar al-Assad.
One of the group’s previous attempts involved the so-called underwear bomber in 2009. In that case, Umar Farouk Abdulmutallab, a Nigerian, tried to detonate an Asiri-built bomb that had been sewn into his underwear. Mr. Abdulmutallab, whose lap caught fire when the plane was in the air, was subdued by passengers before the bomb was able to cause substantial damage. After the airliner landed in Detroit, he was taken into custody by law enforcement officers and was later
sentenced to life in prison
.
Billy Vincent, former security chief for the Federal Aviation Administration, said the new plan for electronic devices could deter attacks. But he said that not all such devices are alike, and the screening would need to reflect those discrepancies.
“A phone is one thing, but with a computer it’s different,” Mr. Vincent said. “You have them turn it on, fine. But what have you accomplished?” In larger devices, he said, there is enough space available for explosives without affecting their power.
Erik Hansen, the senior policy director for the
United States Travel Association
, an industry trade group, said passengers would adapt to the changes.
“In the post-9/11 world, travelers have become used to new security procedures,” he said. “They will accept those changes as long as T.S.A. and D.H.S. provide them with the necessary info.”

Start
The adventure of new ventures.
NoChains
Rich Winley: “We just didn’t have enough data for the app.”
Last July, we
wrote about a start-up
, NoChains, with a free app designed to help users find the best independent, nonchain restaurants in a particular city.
Introduced in Greenville, S.C., in February 2013, the app debuted in New York City in June, briefly hitting No. 50 in iTune’s food and drink category. At that point, the company had raised a small round of investment capital — less than $40,000 — and Rich Winley, a co-founder, was looking to raise as much as $500,000 more to expand into other cities.
The idea behind NoChains was that consumers would search for specific dishes, not restaurants. Rather than searching for “Best Thai food,” for example, they would look for the best pad Thai or tom kha gai. Mr. Winley’s biggest challenge was to build up the app’s database by getting a critical mass of users to “like” and review specific dishes at independent restaurants.
After our post ran, however, Mr. Winley took NoChains to Austin, Tex., and problems began to appear. “We just didn’t have enough data for the app,” he said. NoChains needed a better way to update menu information automatically, and it needed more user-generated content, those “likes” and reviews. There simply weren’t enough people using the app and contributing information to make it relevant.
In addition, Mr. Winley said, consumers weren’t using the app the way he and his co-founder, Dan Mall, envisioned. Instead of searching for a specific dish — and then matching it with a restaurant — they were searching for restaurants first, by cuisine or by dietary needs. After they found a restaurant, they would then search for dishes.
Although Mr. Mall designed the app’s interface, he is not a developer. That meant that every time a new iteration of the app was needed, Mr. Winley had to contract out the work, which limited his ability to respond quickly to customer feedback.
It also was expensive. “You really need a technical co-founder or have to know how to do web development yourself or have enough money to contract it out, especially for consumer apps, which reiterate frequently,” he said. “What we ultimately needed to do was change the functionality, and that would have required us to gut the app, start over and spend a lot of money.”
Had he been able to start over, Mr. Winley would have encouraged users to search for and give input about the best new restaurants in their community, rather than the best dishes.
In January, with the number of downloads dwindling and the company unable to raise more money, the founders took the app out of the iTunes store. They have put the business on hold while they work on other projects.
Mr. Winley was hired by
the Iron Yard
, an organization based in Greenville, S.C., that offers  accelerator and code education programs. He developed NoChains in the Iron Yard’s Greenville accelerator, and he will serve as campus director for the organization’s four Texas locations. The Iron Yard is opening intensive code courses nationwide this year to teach people how to code in three months.
In Texas, the campuses will be in Houston, Austin, Dallas and San Antonio. “The classes draw a lot of career transition folks and entrepreneurs,” Mr. Winley said. “We encourage all of the students to try and work on or for a start-up.
“And you never know,” he continued, “maybe someone coming through one of these academies will want to work on NoChains at some point. I think when I’m ready to start working on it again, it won’t be a tough challenge to find a developer here that wants to work with me.”
You can follow Eilene Zimmerman on
Twitter
.
